(European Biodiversity Partnership) Biodiversa+
Academic and research
France
Charlotte Le Delliou
charlotte.le-delliou@developpement-durable.gouv.fr; anna.sandor@fondationbiodiversite.fr; elisa.dageville@fondationbiodiversite.fr
Development of a Biodiversa+ “Global Biodiversity Framework Action Plan”

Biodiversa+ – the European Biodiversity Partnership – developed its “Global Biodiversity Framework Action Plan” (2026-2028) to answer the question “How will it support the implementation of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework?”. Building upon a 20-year-old network, Biodiversa+ forms a unique partnership of 80+ partners (mainly research programming and funding organisations, ministries and agencies in charge of the environment) from 40+ countries (in Europe and beyond), with a budget of >800 million euros over 7 years. The Partnership's activities range from funding excellent and impactful research to supporting biodiversity monitoring and an enhanced dialogue between decision-makers and experts. Activities are implemented based on the Partnership's strategic document, defining four flagships (i.e., biodiversity protection and restoration, transnational monitoring, Nature-based Solutions and transformative change). Activities are rolled out as part of the five Biodiversa+ work packages, including on the internationalisation of European expertise. Since its launch in 2021, Biodiversa+ has put a particular emphasis on the scientific, technical and technological support to international commitments, especially the ones made as part of the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD). Biodiversa+ seeks an enhanced collaboration between European research and the CBD, via a dedicated workstream as well as the so-called mainstreaming of CBD and KMGBF-related issues across its wide portfolio of activities. Biodiversa+ “Global Biodiversity Framework Action Plan” is compiling more than 25 commitments towards the KMGBF. Three categories of commitments are considered: (1) mainstreaming and capacity-building, 2) knowledge development and exchange, and 3) monitoring and reporting. In total, through its various activities, Biodiversa+ supports the implementation of the four KMGBF goals, manifold targets and indicators as well as the Framework's supporting decisions (e.g., Monitoring Framework).

Accountability Counsel
Non-governmental
United States of America
Gregory Berry
gregory@accountabilitycounsel.org; gregory@accountabilitycounsel.org; gregory@accountabilitycounsel.org

Our commitment to insist that international finance institutions (a) respect human rights in the pursuit of nature-positive impacts, and (b) equip their respective independent accountability mechanisms with an ability to receive complaints discreetly related to biodiversity impacts.

Accountability Counsel commits to pursue improved accountability mechanisms that are responsive to adverse biodiversity impacts enabled by international finance institutions (IFIs), as well as adverse human rights impacts in pursuit of biodiversity targets established by the Kunming - Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF).
As upstream enabling actors, IFIs have a major role in addressing the biodiversity crisis both by directing resources to accelerate efforts toward halting and reversing biodiversity loss, and also by requiring that safeguards be applied to the projects they finance to help avoid negative impacts to critical ecosystems.[1] Independent Accountability Mechanisms (IAMs) established by IFIs are intended to underpin compliance with the environmental and social safeguards applied to every project, and to facilitate meaningful response to risks and remedy for harm.[2]
IAMs can be effective to deliver on biodiversity responsibilities only to the extent that they are enabled to do so. When effectively designed, they can provide substantive and meaningful remedy that advances the targets of the GBF.[3] Adverse biodiversity impacts enabled by internationally-financed projects have presented particular challenges for resolution through IAM processes. With the exception of a few outliers, most IAM policies require that biodiversity concerns be brought only in relation to specific harms directly faced by communities; that is to say that complainants must articulate how specific threats to biodiversity affect them directly.[4] This makes it difficult to bring concerns strictly regarding biodiversity impacts, as most IAMs do not find complaints eligible when project-impacted people are not also directly harmed.[5]
More broadly, people need to know that IAMs exist as channels to alert IFIs about their environmental and social performance.[6] More resources must be dedicated to spread the word and educate communities about these important tools. To that regard, Accountability Counsel commits to support education and empowerment with our Accountability Toolkit, available at https://accountability.tools/, one-stop platform for tools, data, and insights to hold banks and companies accountable for harm they cause to people and the planet. We call on IFI leadership to likewise increase their outreach efforts to spread awareness about IAMs.
IFIs must take responsibility for their role in the biodiversity crisis. They must step up by implementing policy changes that actually allow their respective IAMs to address adverse biodiversity impacts.[7] Accordingly, Accountability Counsel commits to pursue advocacy to improve mechanisms of accountability at DFIs to help halt and reverse biodiversity loss. We urge the signatories ofthe Global Biodiversity Framework to support us in these commitments by using their constituency positions to call for policy reforms that enable IAMs, both by policy and resources, to help banks better appreciate and respond to adverse biodiversity impacts. ___________________________________________________________________________________________________ [1] See, Friends of the Earth, et al., How Should Financiers Align with the Global Biodiversity Framework? Five Key Principles (2023), https://forestsandfinance.org/publications/civil-society-calls-on-both-public-and-private-financial-institutions-to-align-with-the-global-biodiversity-framework/; IPBES, Global Assessment Report on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services (2019), https://www.ipbes.net/global-assessment. ASR Real Estate
Business or industry
Netherlands (Kingdom of the)
Isa Beauchampet
isa.beauchampet@asr.nl
Development of a method for preserving and restoring nature on and around existing real estate

(a) a.s.r. real estate is creating a method in collaboration with Sweco (architecture and engineering consultancy firm) for mapping buildings (also known as ‘green flagging’) where the company can make a positive impact on the conservation and restoration of nature. The results of this method are used for developing a nature-positive strategy for the real estate portfolio. The aim is to ensure that a.s.r. real estate reduces its negative impact and makes a positive contribution to nature through the exploitation of existing real estate. We are currently at an advanced stage with other market initiatives and knowledge institutes to share our experiences to deploy this broadly, because this method can serve as a generic solution that could potentially lead to a market standard for the real estate sector in the Netherlands. The method consists of three phases: Baseline measurement: mapping and quantifying the share of existing vegetation in the real estate portfolioGreen potential in the real estate portfolio: quantifying the amount of unused surface area with a green potential per building for ground level, roof area, and facade areaIdentifying ecological (green) amenities that contribute to the conservation and improvement of local biodiversity(b) The main driver of biodiversity loss we are seeking to address is that of land use change. The construction of real estate in the past has perhaps converted once green land to built environment. On the other hand, it can be that real estate has been developed on already concrete surfaces, but that there is an opportunity through the development of natural surfaces on and around this existing real estate, to achieve a net positive result on nature creation. In both ways, the halting of biodiversity loss or contributing to biodiversity gain relates to land use. More indirect drivers of biodiversity loss we are seeking to address are human population dynamics and economic incentives resulting in environmental harm. In The Netherlands, there is more demand for housing than supply, which is driving up land and real estate prices. Moreover, a green and biodiverse living environment is not always well priced in, or tends to be the first thing that is cut back on when project funding runs low. These factors can hinder the incorporation of nature elements in new building projects, let alone in renovation projects. By creating a practical method for identifying nature-opportunities, nature-inclusivity can become an integrated aspect of real estate design, management and renovations.

BirdLife International
BLI
United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
Julia Keipi
julia.keipi@birdlife.org
Assessing extinction risk of the world’s birds

BirdLife is designated by IUCN as the Red List Authority for birds on the IUCN Red List, meaning that we provide all the information on birds for the IUCN Red List. Our assessments show that one in eight of the world’s birds (1,256 species) is threatened with extinction, including 216 that are classified as Critically Endangered. In combination with 967 species that are classified as Near Threatened, this means that one in five of the world’s birds are of conservation concern.
Figure 1. Proportion of the world’s birds (n=11,185 species) in different Red List categories.
All birds have been assessed for the Red List Analysis eight times since 1988 (a longer and more frequent evaluation than for any other taxonomic group). Analysis of these data enables generation of the Red List Index, which shows that the survival prospects of the world’s birds continues to decline as they move ever closer towards extinction. Trends from 2022-2004 do not suggest that we are on track to reduce extinction risk as committed to in Goal A and Target 4, but the next comprehensive assessment of birds in 2029-2030 will provide a clearer picture.
Figure 2. Red List Index of species survival for the world’s birds 1988-2024, with examples at different time-points illustrating species that have qualified for higher or lower categories of extinction risk owing to genuine deterioration or improvement in their status (Red List category changes resulting from improved knowledge or revised taxonomy are excluded from the trends). No bird species are known yet to have undergone a genuine improvement in their status during 2020-2024 sufficient to qualify for a lower Red List category as a result of successful conservation action.

GBF-INDICATOR-A.1
Helping businesses through the Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool (IBAT) to assess biodiversity risks and opportunities

IBAT provides fast, easy access to global biodiversity data and reports, helping businesses assess biodiversity risks and opportunities, inform sustainability strategies, and meet key disclosure requirements. IBAT provides commercial access to the world’s most authoritative datasets; the World Database on Protected and Conserved Areas (WDPCA), World database of Key Biodiversity Areas (WDKBA) and the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species. We also provide derived datasets - the Rarity-Weighted Richness layer and Species Threat Abatement and Restoration or STAR metric. It is free to create an account in IBAT for all governments, individuals and organisations. Organisations within the private sector are required to pay for more advanced access whilst not-for-profit organisations and research institutions can receive this advanced access for free.

Identification of areas of particular importance for biodiversity (Key Biodiversity Areas: KBAs), and tracking their coverage by protected areas and OECMs

As described in CBD/COP/16/INF/3 (Revised guidance on using the indicators of the monitoring framework), Key Biodiversity Areas (KBAs) represent the most comprehensive standardised dataset of nationally identified areas of particular importance for biodiversity. BirdLife uses data from the World Database of Key Biodiversity Areas in combination with data on protected areas and OECMs from Protected Planet (managed by UNEP-WCMC) to generate SDG indicators 14.5.1 (marine), 15.1.2 (terrestrial and freshwater), and 15.4.1(mountains) tracking coverage of KBAs by protected areas and OECMs for all countries each year. The latest analysis (January 2026) shows that on average, 44.4% of each KBA is covered by protected areas and OECMs, with 20.3% completely covered, 42.6% partially covered, and 37.1% with no coverage. Between 2022 and 2025, mean coverage has increased from 44 to 44.4%. Continuation of this rate of growth would lead to mean coverage of 45.1% by 2030, falling well short of effectively conserving all areas of particular importance for biodiversity by 2030. KBA classificationMean % area coveragePercentage KBAs with complete coverage (%)Percentage KBAs with partial coverage (%)Percentage KBAs with no coverage (%)All44.4020.3142.5637.13Terrestrial45.1320.5143.0536.44Freshwater44.1218.8844.5336.59Marine46.6923.9541.1134.94
Case study: The Tropical Andes feature extraordinary diversity and a high degree of endemism of freshwater species, although conservation prioritization efforts have mainly focused on terrestrial species. Priority conservation sites have not included these freshwater species because baseline information on their status and distribution has been lacking. In 2022, the KBA Partnership launched a project to map a comprehensive network of KBAs across the Tropical Andes. Despite their status as biodiversity hotspots and globally significant carbon sinks, these regions lacked detailed inventories of the most important sites for nature. To address this knowledge gap, the project “Key Biodiversity Areas – establishing the blueprint for 30x30” was initiated, led by BirdLife International on behalf of the KBA Partnership and funded by the Bezos Earth Fund. Thirty-nine of the KBAs were existing KBAs as they were previously identified for terrestrial species, 22 were existing protected areas, and 25 were newly delineated KBAs. The identification and mapping of these areas have helped bring freshwater species into focus, generating essential baseline data that will underpin future site-level conservation planning and action.

GBF-INDICATOR-2.CY.2
Avian Sensitivity Tool for Energy Planning (AVISTEP)

Tackling the climate emergency requires a rapid, worldwide transition to renewable energy technologies. However, such a comprehensive reshaping of our global energy infrastructure will pose significant threats to biodiversity if not planned responsibly. To address this, BirdLife International has developed AVISTEP - the Avian Sensitivity Tool for Energy Planning to identify where renewable energy could impact birds and should therefore be avoided. Equipped with this information, those involved in planning renewable energy will be able to ensure that facilities are developed in the most appropriate locations. The assessment of avian sensitivity presented in AVISTEP is intended to provide a landscape-scale understanding of the potential risks posed to birds by certain types of energy infrastructure. Such an evaluation of spatial risk will undoubtedly improve early-stage renewable energy planning. However, there is an imperfect knowledge of avian distribution and an incomplete understanding of the factors that make certain bird species more susceptible to impacts from energy infrastructure. By design, the assessments are precautionary and intended to provide an awareness of the at-risk bird species that could be present within an area and what such a species composition might mean for the development of renewable energy and power lines. This information is intended to inform, rather than replace, subsequent site-scale evaluation. In addition, the assessments take account of areas of general importance for nature, such as Protected Areas and wildlife-rich landcover types. AVISTEP provides users with spatial heat maps depicting potential avian sensitivity in relation to the following types of energy infrastructure: onshore wind, offshore wind, solar photovoltaic, transmission powerlines (long-distance, high voltage) and distribution powerlines (short-distance, low- and medium-voltage). Each 5km x 5km cell within the heatmaps has a sensitivity score. AVISTEP data is currently available for Australia, Egypt, India, Kenya, Laos, Nepal, Thailand, Uzbekistan, and Vietnam. For example, AVISTEP data informed the Strategic Environmental and Social Assessment (SESA) of renewable energy projects in Uzbekistan, conducted by the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD), contributing to the national goal of 25 gigawatts of solar and wind capacity by 2030.

BUND e.V.
Germany
Nicola Uhde
nicola.uhde@bund.net
European Green Belt Initiative

The European Green Belt Initiative (EGBI) is a pan-European, long-running conservation commitment that has been developed continuously since the early 2000s—with the idea publicly expressed in 2002 and the pan-European initiative taking shape in 2003—and has since matured into a durable cross-border cooperation platform. It focuses on the former Iron Curtain border zone, where decades of restricted access created a “living memorial landscape” and an exceptional chain of habitats. Today, the European Green Belt stretches over 12,500 km across 24 countries, from the Barents Sea through the Baltic, Central Europe and the Balkans to the Black and Adriatic Seas. (a) Scope and actions proposed EGBIs scope is to safeguard, restore and reconnect this transcontinental ecological network while integrating cultural heritage and community benefits. Core actions promoted through the initiative include: (1) supporting nature conservation along the corridor; (2) influencing policy and planning so the Green Belt is reflected in decisions by authorities and other sectors; and (3) connecting people through awareness, participation and cross-border exchange. Implementation is enabled by a structured governance model with Regional Coordinators and National Focal Points that stimulate projects, convene stakeholders and strengthen cooperation across borders. (b) Drivers and enabling conditions of biodiversity loss addressed EGBI targets major direct drivers in the corridor, especially habitat loss, degradation and fragmentation driven by land-use intensification and infrastructure that disrupts connectivity. Its emphasis on protected-area cooperation, gap-closing measures, and locally led restoration financed through BESTbelt directly supports reconnecting landscapes and improving habitat condition. It also tackles indirect drivers/enabling conditions that often determine outcomes: fragmented governance across borders, inconsistent priorities, limited local capacity and finance, and low awareness. EGBI’s long operational history is a key added value here—providing institutional continuity, trusted networks and repeatable collaboration routines that persist beyond short project cycles. Contribution to the UN Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) EGBI advances the CBD’s objectives—conservation, sustainable use, and enabling conditions for equitable governance—by operationalising ecological networks at continental scale. Under the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework, it most directly contributes to Target 3, supports Target 2, reinforces Target 1, and aligns with Target 22 through its participation- and community-oriented approach.

ReCo - Restore to Connect the European Green Belt in Central Europe

ReCo (“Restore to Connect”) is an Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE project that improves biodiversity and ecological connectivity along the Central European section of the European Green Belt. It combines GIS/geo-information and data processing to map habitat-network gaps and prioritise restoration sites, with on-the-ground pilot actions and local restoration plans co-developed with authorities, landowners and NGOs. A community-based approach helps mobilise local stewardship and co-funding, while outputs such as a Transnational Restoration & Connectivity Strategy (“toolbox”) and practitioners’ guides support replication. (Interreg Central Europe) ReCo targets key direct drivers of biodiversity loss in the corridor—land-use change and pressure, fragmentation, and altered water regimes—through wetland/stream restoration, grassland and meadow management, removal of overgrowth or non-native vegetation, and species-focused monitoring (e.g., freshwater pearl mussel, wildcat, bison). Indirect drivers/enabling conditions are addressed by strengthening cross-border coordination, spatial planning and the shared evidence base for decision-making. (Interreg Central Europe) In CBD terms, the project supports the Convention’s objectives on conservation and sustainable use and is closely aligned with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework, especially Target 2 on restoring degraded ecosystems and Target 3 on well-connected, effectively managed protected and conserved areas. (Convention on Biological Diversity)

Ecosystems are conserved, extinctions are halted and genetic diversity is maintained
GBF-INDICATOR-304
Tomorrow´s wildcat forests

The European wildcat (Felis silvestris) lives in structurally diverse deciduous and mixed deciduous forests. Originally native throughout Germany, today around 5,000 to 7,000 individuals remain, mainly in central and southwestern Germany. The wildcat is classified as “endangered” on Germany’s Red List of Animals, Plants and Fungi. In recent decades, the species has been rediscovered in many areas where it had long been considered extinct. However, it is still absent from large parts of Germany and Central Europe. As Germany represents the global center of the wildcat’s distribution, the country bears a particular international responsibility for its conservation. Like hardly any other species, the wildcat serves as a flagship species for nature conservation and for maintaining connected forest habitats. Wildcats are highly sensitive to the fragmentation of their habitats by roads, settlements, and cleared agricultural landscapes. Where wildcats occur, conditions are also favourable for other endangered species such as the lynx, for shy bird species like the black stork, as well as for rare beetle species and fungi. This is why BUND (Friends of the Earth Germany) is redesigning forest sites in ten federal states. Our aim is to encourage the wildcats’ successful reproduction there and allow them to recolonize other areas. To implement the project, BUND is cooperating with forest users and decision-makers in the fields of forestry, agriculture and hunting as well as with land owners, administrations, municipalities and the church. Specifically, the project enhances forest edges and develops adjacent, cover-rich open areas in a way that is suitable for wildcats. In managed forests, structural diversity is also to be improved, for example by increasing the proportion of deadwood, creating brushwood piles from tree crowns, and permanently leaving stacked timber and upturned root plates in place. Monitoring of wildcat populations is also part of the project: using so-called lure sticks, the further recolonization of the wildcat is being studied and documented in the peripheral areas of its range. By increasingly involving volunteers in the project, BUND aims to raise awareness and inspire more people to support wildcat and biodiversity conservation. The project is being jointly implemented by the federal level BUND, its youth branch BUNDjugend and the BUND regional associations in Baden-Württemberg, Bavaria, Brandenburg, Hesse, Lower Saxony, North Rhine Westphalia, Saarland, Saxony, Saxony-Anhalt and Thuringia. As part of the Federal Biodiversity Programme, the project is funded by the Federal Agency for Nature Conservation (BfN) with resources from the Federal Ministry for the Environment, Climate Protection, Nature Conservation and Nuclear Safety (BMUKN) from October 2022 to October 2028.

Ensure participation, justice, and rights for indigenous peoples and local communities, women, youth persons with disabilities and environmental defenders
The Safety Net for the Wildcat

The fragmentation of habitats endangers biodiversity and particularly affects forest ecosystems. The Bund für Umwelt- und Naturschutz Deutschland (BUND e. V.) has therefore been working for many years through its project “Wildcat Rescue Network” to reconnect forest habitats. By planting green corridors, a nationwide network of interconnected forest habitats is being created across Germany. The wildcat benefits from this initiative, along with a wide range of other endangered species. The European wildcat (Felis silvestris silvestris) was once widespread throughout Central Europe but had nearly become extinct by the beginning of the 20th century due to human persecution. Starting from a few remaining populations in central and western Germany, it has managed to recolonize many of its former habitats in recent decades. However, the wildcat is still largely absent from some regions of Germany. Wildcats prefer near-natural, structurally diverse deciduous and mixed forests with plenty of deadwood, and they particularly use clearings and forest edges adjacent to meadows for hunting. They are strongly dependent on areas that provide sufficient cover. Intensively used agricultural landscapes lacking structural elements such as copses or hedgerows often present insurmountable barriers for wildcats. In addition, Germany’s remaining forest habitats are fragmented by one of the densest road networks in the world. For many threatened species such as the Eurasian otter, the wolf, and the wildcat, road traffic is now the most frequent cause of death. Settlements and ongoing land consumption further contribute to landscape fragmentation. Friends of the Earth Germany (BUND) have been dedicated to wildcat conservation for over 30 years. Since 2004, the organization has bundled its activities in what is now 13 federal states under the project “Wildcat Rescue Network” (Rettungsnetz Wildkatze). The focus lies on creating green corridors between isolated forest areas, enhancing forest habitats, and conducting public outreach work. The aim of the project is to reconnect isolated forest areas by planting green migration corridors made up of trees and shrubs. In the long term, a continuous network of interconnected forest habitats is to be established—through direct planting activities, but above all through advocacy work to ensure the concrete integration of biotope connectivity planning into official spatial planning processes. The scientific basis of the project is the Wildcat Pathways Plan (http://wildkatzenwegeplan.de), which identifies optimal connection routes between existing wildcat forests and forests that are potentially suitable habitats but have not yet been colonized. The wildcat serves as the flagship species of the project. However, forest connectivity and the enrichment of agricultural landscapes benefit not only the wildcat but also numerous other endangered species. The first forest corridor was planted in 2007 in Thuringia. This was followed by 31 additional corridor plantings by BUND in Thuringia, Lower Saxony, Hesse, Saxony-Anhalt, Saxony, Baden-Württemberg and Rhineland-Palatinate. A key prerequisite for the successful implementation of such measures is constructive cooperation between authorities, landowners, land users, and local conservation organizations. Another important component of the “Wildcat Rescue Network” is advocacy work for green bridges and other wildlife crossings over heavily trafficked roads. Since 2011, BUND, in cooperation with the Senckenberg Society for Nature Research, has established a comprehensive monitoring program as part of the project “Wildcat Leap” (Wildkatzensprung), successfully involving volunteers, and has developed a wildcat genotype database. Wildcats are monitored using so-called lure sticks, which are wooden sticks treated with valerian. The animals are attracted by the smell of the valerian and rub their fur against the wood. Later on, citizen scientists collect the hair from the lure sticks for genetic analysis. This initiative was funded by the German Federal Agency for Nature Conservation within the framework of the Federal Biological Diversity Programme. The wildcat monitoring has been continued since the end of this project in 2017. To date, more than 4000 wildcat hair samples from more than 1500 individuals have been collected with the help of several hundred volunteers.

GBF-T03. 30% of areas are effectively conserved; GBF-T04. Threatened species are recovering, genetic diversity is being maintained and human-wildlife conflict is being managed; GBF-T10. In Search of the Garden Dormouse

Four dormouse species are native to Germany: the edible dormouse (Glis glis), the hazel dormouse (Muscardinus avellanarius), the forest dormouse (Dryomys nitedula), of which no current records exist in Germany, and the garden dormouse (Eliomys quercinus), the focus of this project. The garden dormouse is endemic to Europe and the area of its distribution is smaller than that of other European dormouse species. The species has undergone significant range contraction during the last 100 years, and from around 1970 onwards this has continued at an increasing rate. The garden dormouse has already disappeared from several European countries, and in others it is on the brink of extinction (Bertolino 2017). The decline is also significant in Germany (Meinig et al. 2023, Büchner et al. 2024): in the current German Red List, the garden dormouse is classified as ‘endangered’. Germany is largely responsible for the worldwide conservation of the garden dormouse, as a large proportion of the world population lives here. For many endangered species in Germany, the causes of their decline are roughly known but in the case of the garden dormouse, the reasons were still a mystery until recently. It occurs in very different habitats. In Germany for example, they can be found in rather inhospitable spruce forests such as the summit of the Brocken in the Harz Mountains or in the high altitudes of the Black Forest. However, it is also native to the Rhine and Moselle region and is found in vineyards and also in several cities such as Mannheim, Mainz, Wiesbaden, Koblenz, Trier, Bonn, and Cologne. How can a species that is so adaptable and can use such different habitats be so endangered? What measures are required to protect it? These questions became the starting point for the nationwide project ‘In Search of the Garden Dormouse’ that took place between 2018 and 2024. In this joint project, Friends of the Earth Germany (Bund für Umwelt und Naturschutz Deutschland, BUND), the Conservation Genetics Group at Senckenberg Research Institute (Senckenberg Gesellschaft für Naturforschung, SGN) and the Justus-Liebig University Giessen (JLU) dedicated themselves to the protection of the garden dormouse. The project was funded by the German Federal Agency for Nature Conservation (Bundesamt für Naturschutz, BfN) with funds from the German Federal Ministry for the Environment (Bundesministerium für Umwelt, Naturschutz, nukleare Sicherheit und Verbraucherschutz, BMU) and other funding bodies from the federal states. Initially, the focus centred on three questions: What is the current distribution of the garden dormouse? What are the species’ habitat requirements? What are the causes for the population decline? Based on the outcome of these findings, concrete measures were be developed and successively tested, supplemented, and further developed in order to prevent the extinction of the garden dormouse in Germany. ‘In Search of the Garden Dormouse’ took place with the participation of numerous volunteers as a citizen science project. Although a nationwide project, it focused regionally was on the federal states of Baden-Württemberg, Bavaria, Hesse, Lower Saxony, North Rhine-Westphalia, Rhineland-Palatinate, and Thuringia. Teams of volunteers, students, researchers, and nature conservationists have been working on the following topics, among others, to address the unanswered questions about the biology of the garden dormouse and threats to its survival: • Genetics: Establishment of a genetic marker set for garden dormice in order to investigate the genetic diversity of populations and to be able to delimit them geographically • Diseases and causes of death: Collection of carcasses and veterinary examinations of garden dormice for causes of death, diseases, parasites, and pollutants • Food: Analysis of diet based on faecal samples and stomach contents Observation of garden dormice using telemetry with regard to habitat selection, space use, and behaviourLong-term monitoring of garden dormouse activity over an extended period at selected locations using specially developed equipment Recording the phenology of the species.Observations of garden dormice were recorded using an online reporting tool and by particularly dedicated volunteers who used footprint tunnels, wildlife cameras, and nest boxes and collected samples for genetic analyses. All of the data were entered into a project database, which at the end of the project in August 2024 contained around 22,000 data records. During the project, citizens were able to submit their observations of garden dormice across Germany via the online reporting tool. One central outcome of the project was a handbook of the experiences and findings of this six-year project. It is intended to be a practical guide and to show: • Where garden dormice can be found, • Which factors pose a threat to the species, • What knowledge about the habitats and the biology is relevant for the conservation of the species, • How garden dormice (and other dormice) can be reliably detected, and • How the species can be protected. The ‘In Search of the Garden Dormouse’ project has laid the foundations for the protection of the garden dormouse in Germany. In addition, the project made a general contribution to the conservation of biodiversity, as many of the measures proposed here also help many other species. The activities of the project are continued since the end of the funded project on a lower level. Furthermore, the outcomes of the project are used for the planning of upcoming projects on the conservation of the garden dormouse.
GBF-T04. Threatened species are recovering, genetic diversity is being maintained and human-wildlife conflict is being managed
Lynx Thuringia – Connecting Europe’s Lynx Populations

The lynx once inhabited all major forest regions and low mountain ranges across Europe. However, due to habitat loss, the decline of prey species, and intensive persecution, the lynx disappeared from Germany and large parts of Central Europe in the 19th century. The last lynx in the Thuringian Forest was killed in 1843. Today, the few lynx in Germany only live here thanks to targeted reintroduction projects. There are now populations in the Bavarian Forest, the Harz Mountains, and the Palatinate Forest. Nevertheless, the lynx remains very rare and is classified as “critically endangered” on Germany’s Red List. The decisive factor for this classification is the lack of genetic exchange between the populations, which are located far apart from one another. What is still missing is the connecting link — a stable lynx population in the Thuringian Forest to serve as a bridge between the populations in the Bavarian Forest and the Harz Mountains. Due to its natural conditions, the Thuringian Forest offers suitable habitat for lynx. However, even 20 years after the reintroduction in the Harz, the species has not succeeded in recolonizing this area. Systematic studies on lynx distribution (monitoring) and computer simulations indicate that, without support, a reproducing lynx population is unlikely to establish itself in the Thuringian Forest in the foreseeable future. The project “Lynx Thuringia – Connecting Europe’s Lynx Populations” therefore aims to establish a stable lynx population in the Thuringian Forest by releasing lynx — particularly females. Between 2024 and 2027, up to 20 lynx are to be released. In the long term, these reinforcement measures are intended to connect the previously isolated lynx populations and thus secure the species’ survival in Germany. In addition to research and monitoring, the project focuses on outreach, dialogue, and environmental education — because only when people know the lynx will they understand its importance for stable ecosystems. So far, five lynx have been released: three males and two females. The animals are currently ranging in the central and southern parts of the Thuringian Forest. Two of the lynx were captured from the wild in Romania, while the others were born in captivity. In addition, camera traps have documented three male lynx and one female lynx in southern Thuringia that migrated from northern Bavaria. At the Hütscheroda Wildcat Village, the lynx are prepared for release in a dedicated enclosure, separated from human contact. Public acceptance of the lynx’s return and the release program is promoted through extensive education and outreach efforts, including lectures for hunters, press events, and school projects. “Lynx Thuringia – Connecting Europe’s Lynx Populations” is a joint project of Friends of the Earth Germany (BUND), WWF, ThüringenForst, the Hütscheroda Wildcat Village, the Thuringian State Hunting Association, the UNESCO Thuringian Forest Biosphere Reserve, the Thuringian Forest Nature Park, the University of Göttingen, and the Romanian project partners ACDB and Romsilva. The project is part of the European lynx expert network Linking Lynx, which is dedicated to conserving and connecting lynx populations in Central Europe. Its goal is to establish a stable and self-sustaining lynx population in Central Europe through targeted releases and the connection of existing populations. Many of the lynx released in the Thuringian Forest originate from the conservation breeding program of the European Association of Zoos and Aquaria (EAZA). The project is implemented under the program “Funding for Projects for the Development of Nature and Landscape (ENL)” and is supported by the Thuringian Ministry for the Environment, Energy, Nature Conservation and Forestry. Funding is provided by the Free State of Thuringia as well as by ENL funds from the European Agricultural Fund for Rural Development (EAFRD). Europe and the State of Thuringia are investing here in rural areas.

GBF-G-A. Ecosystems are conserved, extinctions are halted and genetic diversity is maintained; GBF-T14. The multiple values of biodiversity are integrated into decision-making at all levels; GBF-T20. Capacity-building and development, technology transfer, and technical and scientific cooperation for implementation is strengthened
DaRe to Connect (D2C) - Interreg Danube Transnational Programme (20128-2021)

ReCo (“Restore to Connect”) is an Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE project that improves biodiversity and ecological connectivity along the Central European section of the European Green Belt. It combines GIS/geo-information and data processing to map habitat-network gaps and prioritise restoration sites, with on-the-ground pilot actions and local restoration plans co-developed with authorities, landowners and NGOs. A community-based approach helps mobilise local stewardship and co-funding, while outputs such as a Transnational Restoration & Connectivity Strategy (“toolbox”) and practitioners’ guides support replication. ReCo targets key direct drivers of biodiversity loss in the corridor—land-use change and pressure, fragmentation, and altered water regimes—through wetland/stream restoration, grassland and meadow management, removal of overgrowth or non-native vegetation, and species-focused monitoring (e.g., freshwater pearl mussel, wildcat, bison). Indirect drivers/enabling conditions are addressed by strengthening cross-border coordination, spatial planning and the shared evidence base for decision-making. In CBD terms, the project supports the Convention’s objectives on conservation and sustainable use and is closely aligned with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework, especially Target 2 on restoring degraded ecosystems and Target 3 on well-connected, effectively managed protected and conserved areas.

Campaign for Nature
CFN
Georg Schwede
georg@campaignfornature.com; Michael@campaignfornature.com
Report on a fair share of biodiversity finance under Target 19a of the GBF.

New ODI research has found that only two out of 28 developed countries were contributing their fair share of an internationally agreed commitment to provide $20 billion per year in biodiversity finance to developing countries, with most of them needing to at least double their funding to help halt and reverse biodiversity loss across our planet. With just months to go until the agreed deadline, this report is sounding the alarm for an urgent and significant increase in nature funding. The report by the global affairs think tank ODI, commissioned by the Campaign for Nature, is the first study to measure how much each developed country should be paying to developing countries in order to keep the commitment they made in the landmark 2022 Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF). In the GBF, the world agreed to ambitious targets to combat biodiversity loss. To help achieve them, developed countries agreed to contribute collectively at least $20 billion per year to developing countries by 2025. This was elevated to a leader level commitment in the G7 leaders communique last week. The report’s calculations took into account countries’ historical impact on biodiversity, their ability to pay and population size in calculating what they should pay. The report utilised the most recent official public OECD data. The report’s findings articulate what ambition on biodiversity finance must look like for each developed country. It found that Norway and Sweden were the only countries providing more than their fair share, Germany and France were providing 99% and 92% respectively and Australia was at 74% of its fair share. The largest dollar gaps were in Japan, the UK, Italy, Canada, Korea and Spain. Together, they account for 71% of the aggregate shortfall. This report lays out how the gap can be closed before time runs out. The report - “A fair share of biodiversity finance: Apportioning responsibility for the $20 billion target by 2025” - comes as experts find biodiversity loss accelerating at an unprecedented rate. Almost half of all species on earth are undergoing population declines, with up to one million species facing possible extinction, many within decades, a rate never seen before in human history. Successfully addressing this crisis will depend on whether the world invests enough to halt biodiversity collapse. This annual $20 billion by 2025 agreement is the first test, the report says. In response to the report, Campaign for Nature has set out recommendations, including calling on: Developed countries, who are responsible for delivering the annual $20 billion, to: Urgently increase funding to meet their fair share to developing countries and ensure they reach the collective target by 2025; Immediately launch a ministerial level initiative to coordinate the delivery of this funding;Mobilize private resources through regulation and incentives, which alongside public finance and philanthropic contributions can help meet the $20 billion target;Wealthy countries who are not members of the OECD Development Assistance Committee cited in the report should make substantial contributions, voluntarily assuming financing responsibilities to developing countries as agreed in the GBF;The United States, which is not a party to the UN Convention on Biological Diversity, but has the largest economy in the world and the largest historical biodiversity footprint, to urgently and significantly increase its international nature finance. Campaign for Nature argues that while some countries have contributed more since the data was recorded, there is no official tally of those contributions and they are unlikely to have been substantial enough to materially move the needle. Progress on this $20 billion promise is now critical to building trust between the Global North and South for the upcoming nature COP16 in Colombia, as much of the world's remaining biodiversity is located in developing countries who will bear a disproportionate share of the responsibility and costs of saving precious biomes. Furthermore, Campaign for Nature outlines that increasing funding to protect global biodiversity isn’t an act of charity, it is an obligation developed countries took as part of a commitment by all countries to increase nature finance to close the biodiversity gap. The sum of $20 billion is a relatively small but important contribution to developing countries’ stewardship of the biological resources and ecosystem services that the global community depends on. Investment is crucial for safeguarding a liveable planet as the continued loss of nature will have serious implications for food and water supplies, our climate, disease outbreaks, as well as peace and security globally. Campaign for Nature further emphasizes that there is a high economic return on investment in nature. According to the World Economic Forum, half of global GDP ($44 trillion per year) is dependent on nature. There is also a cost to inaction. Analysis from the OECD finds that from 1997 to 2011, the world lost between $4-20 trillion every year due to factors such as lower crop yields, fewer fish catches, higher costs due to flooding, and loss of potential new sources of medicine. A coalition of non-governmental, Indigenous and youth organizations recently launched a ‘$20 billion x 25 campaign’, calling on developed country parties, and countries voluntarily assuming developed country parties’ obligations, to urgently accelerate efforts to increase finance for developing country parties.
GBF-T19. Financial resources increased to $200 billion per year, including $ 30 billion through international finance
Report and interactive dashboard detailing international funding for target 3 of the GBF

Report and interactive dashboard detailing international funding for Target 3 of the GBF. Description of commitmentBackground Indufor an independent consultancy, has produced the first systematic analysis of international public and philanthropic funding for 30x30 in developing countries Target 3 calls for the effective conservation and management of at least 30 percent of the world’s lands, inland waters, coastal areas, and oceans by 2030, known as “30x30”.Effective designation and management of Protected and Conserved Areas (PCAs) are therefore a critical measure of our global progress towards safeguarding nature. International biodiversity finance plays a critical role in meeting 30x30 International biodiversity finance is money that moves from developed countries to Global South countries and; directly funds a portion of the costs of PCAs in developing countriescatalyzes increased domestic funding of PCAs in developing countries andbuilds trust between developed and developing countriesDeveloped countries agreed to provided at least US$ 30 billion of international biodiversity finance annually by 2025 and at least US$ 30 billion by 2030 to meet all the goals of the GBF International biodiversity funding totaled $16.8 billion in 2023 (OECD) towards all targets in the GBF. It is estimated that 20% of total biodiversity finance should go towards achieving the 30x30 target specifically.This would mean donor countries providing $4bn in biodiversity finance by 2025 and $6bn by 2030 to support developing countries to meet the 30x30 target. Tracking international funding for 30x30: a first of its kind report This report is the first analysis of international biodiversity funding specifically for 30x30.The report includes 30x30 funding from bilateral, multilateral and philanthropic funders.The report measures 30x30 finance from developed to developing countries but does not measure domestic finance in developing or developed countries.Report Highlights The report finds that developed countries are currently (2024) providing just over $1 billion per year for protected and conserved areas in developing countriesThe Kunming-Montreal GBF appears to have catalyzed a sharp increase in international funding for PCAs:However, even with this growth in funding to 2024, the increased amount still fails far short of the total international biodiversity funding necessary for developing countries to implement 30x30 effectively Donor countries should be providing $4bn p/a by 2025 and $6bn p/a by 2030 to the Global South if 30x30 is to be implemented effectively. This means the 2025 target was being missed by 75% in 2024.The report is based on 2024 data, before cuts to USAID and other donor country aid programmes, including Germany, France and UK were announced in 2025. Virtually all international biodiversity funding is in the form of grants One of the most positive findings of the report is that 97% of funding is provided in grants and only 3% is in loans or other arrangements.This compares to overall international biodiversity funding, in which less than 50% of the total is in the form of grants.Germany is by far the largest funder of 30x30 Germany has disbursed $271 million per year across 55 countries since 2022 – responsible for 25% of the total on its own - and is approximately double the next largest funder.The World Bank, Global Environment Facility (GEF), European Union and United States round out the top five funders.Marine protected areas are receiving significantly less funding than terrestrial PCAs Only 14% of total financial flows are going to support marine protected areas (MPAs) in the Global South.Funding for marine areas has also declined per unit area protected, as the increase in funding has not kept pace with the expansion of MPAs in the Global South. Campaign for Nature's three observations from the report The global economy has a GDP of about US$ 110 trillion in 2025, all of which at least 50% relies on nature to continue to provide essential public goods (food, water, climate). It is therefore eminently affordable and extremely good value for money for the global economy to spend US$4bn p/a on 30x30 by 2025 (representing 0.003% of global GDP)If governments feel they do no have sufficient financial resources available to meet these targets then they have a responsibility to regulate tax and incentivise the private sector particularly those directly associated with the destruction of nature in order to raise sufficient public finance to pay for protected and conserved areas, which provide essential global public goods with a ROI of at least 5:1Governments can raise revenues for nature on the polluter/user pays principle, for example taxes on fossil fuels and other extractive industries, user fees for sectors such as shipping and aviation, and tourism fees on international visitors and cruise ships, with a proportion of revenue raised ringfenced for spending on the protection of natureMain recommendations: Funding for 30x30 should represent at least 20% of total international biodiversity funding, based onestimates that the costs to meet 30x30 represent 20% of the total costs to meet all global biodiversity goals. This should mean that the Global North is spending $4 billion p/a by 2025 and $6bn p/a by 2030 on support for the Global South to achieve 30x30. This means the 2025 target was being missed by 75% in 2024.The report is based on 2024 data, before cuts to USAID and other donor country aid programmes, including Germany, France and UK were announced in 2025. International funding from public donors should be invested in financial mechanisms like the Legacy Landscapes Fund, which provide long-term funding to protected areas in developing countries, to leverage additional financial private sector and philanthropic financial contributions and secure sustained funding for PCA’s. Given the significant climate mitigation and adaptation potential of 30x30, private and public donors should increase funding to 30x30 as part of their climate finance strategies eDx30 as part of their climate finance strategies.Available funding should be directed to countries with the greatest global responsibility for achieving 30x30 and to PCAs with the greatest value for biodiversity and climate change mitigation A materially higher portion of 30x30 funding should go directly to Indigenous people and local communities to support their efforts to meet 30x30Only 14% of current 30x30 funding goes to marine protected areas. This needs to substantially increase if 30% of the global ocean is to be protected by 2030. Most of the international funding must continue to be provided in the form of grants due to the limited opportunities for nature to generate financial returns to pay back loans or earn returns on equity.Recognising the multiple benefits beyond biodiversity and climate that PCAs provide, including a wide range of socio-economic benefits such as jobs, health, food and water security, international funding for 30x30 should increase even in the face of pressures on development budgets. If there is not enough funding available, more should be raised from sectors of the economy that destroy nature (e.g. extractives) or benefit from its resources for free (e.g. tourism).
CBD Women’s Caucus
CBD WC
Women organization
Argentina
Amelia Arreguin Prado
amelia.arreguin@gmail.com; amelia@cbdwomenscaucus.org
Strengthening coordinated implementation of Target 23 and the Gender Plan of Action (2022–2030) through the Gender & Biodiversity Focal Points Network

(a) Overview of scope and actions proposedThe CBD Women’s Caucus commits to facilitating and consolidating the Gender & Biodiversity Focal Points Network as a sustained coordination mechanism to strengthen implementation of Target 23 and the Gender Plan of Action (2022–2030) under the Kunming–Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KM-GBF). The Network provides a dedicated space for officially designated Gender & Biodiversity Focal Points to exchange information, share experiences, identify challenges, and coordinate approaches to advance gender-responsive biodiversity policies at national, regional and global levels. The commitment responds to persistent structural gaps in implementation, including: Uneven integration of gender equality across Parties;Fragmented coordination between ministries responsible for environment and gender;Limited peer-learning opportunities for national Gender Focal Points;Capacity constraints affecting effective participation in CBD processes.The Network supports coherence, mutual learning and sustained political and technical momentum on gender equality across CBD processes. Core Areas of Action The Network contributes to KM-GBF implementation by: Facilitating peer exchange and collective learning Providing regular opportunities for Gender & Biodiversity Focal Points to share national experiences, good practices, and implementation challenges related to Target 23 and gender-responsive NBSAPs.Strengthening coordination across CBD processes Enabling structured reflection on negotiation developments and intersessional opportunities, helping Focal Points maintain visibility of gender commitments across subsidiary bodies and agenda items.Supporting implementation of the Gender Plan of Action (2022–2030) Encouraging alignment between national implementation efforts and global commitments under the Gender Plan of Action and Target 23.Promoting institutional coherence Supporting stronger collaboration between gender institutions and environmental ministries at national level, contributing to enabling conditions under Section C of the KM-GBF.The CBD Women’s Caucus facilitates the Network’s processes and documentation, while respecting the mandates and official positions of participating Focal Points. (b) Drivers and enabling conditions addressed This commitment addresses enabling conditions under Section C by responding to: Institutional fragmentation between gender and biodiversity governance structures;Capacity gaps in national implementation of gender-responsive biodiversity policies;Limited structured coordination mechanisms for Gender Focal Points;Inconsistent visibility of gender equality across CBD processes.By strengthening coordination and coherence, the Network supports durable and systemic implementation of Target 23.
GBF-T23. Implementation follows a gender-responsive approach

GBF-INDICATOR-21.CT.2; GBF-INDICATOR-193
Advancing gender-responsive biodiversity monitoring, sex-disaggregated data and accountability mechanisms for implementation and global review of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework

(a) Overview of scope and actions proposedThe CBD Women’s Caucus commits to strengthening gender-responsive monitoring, reporting and accountability mechanisms under the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KM-GBF), including through the systematic promotion of sex-disaggregated data, gender-sensitive indicators, and community-generated evidence. This commitment supports the implementation of Decision 15/11 (Gender Plan of Action 2023–2030), Decision 15/6 (KM-GBF), and Target 23, and contributes to enabling conditions under Section C of the Framework. The commitment responds to a persistent structural challenge: the invisibility of women’s contributions to biodiversity conservation, sustainable use, land stewardship and traditional knowledge systems within national and global monitoring frameworks. Since 2023, the CBD Women’s Caucus has undertaken the following actions: 1. Technical contributions to the KM-GBF Monitoring FrameworkSubmitted technical inputs to discussions on the KM-GBF monitoring framework.Advocated for sex-disaggregation as an overarching principle across biodiversity indicators.Proposed the integration of gender-sensitive indicators aligned with SDG 5.a.1 and 5.a.2.Contributed policy briefs to SBSTTA 26 discussions on headline indicators and data gaps.Engaged in expert-level discussions concerning methodologies for reporting and financial tracking. 2. Development and dissemination of sample gender-sensitive indicators The Caucus has developed and shared: Sample biodiversity indicators disaggregated by sex;Practical examples such as:Proportion of women and men participating in community-based biodiversity initiatives;Secure tenure rights to land and natural resources disaggregated by sex;Representation of women in biodiversity governance bodies;Participation rates of women in access and benefit-sharing processes.These materials are shared through policy briefs, webinars and internal technical guidance, supporting Parties in integrating gender-responsive indicators into NBSAPs and national reporting processes. 3. Promotion of innovative and complementary data methodologies Recognizing the limitations of official statistics, the commitment promotes: Household-level surveys;Time-use surveys to capture unpaid care and biodiversity-related labour;Citizen-generated and community-based monitoring;Complementary data reflecting Indigenous women’s biodiversity stewardship;Qualitative evidence documenting differentiated impacts of biodiversity loss.These approaches aim to ensure that biodiversity monitoring reflects lived realities and supports equitable policy design. 4. Participation in expert and advisory bodies influencing global review Between 2023–2025, members of the Caucus have contributed gender expertise to: The ad hoc scientific and technical advisory group preparing the Global Report on Collective Progress;Technical and Scientific Cooperation processes;Technical Expert Group on Financial Reporting;Informal Advisory Group on Technical and Scientific Cooperation;Through these engagements, gender considerations are embedded within the evidence base informing global stocktake and implementation review processes. (b) Drivers and enabling conditions addressed This commitment addresses key enabling conditions under Section C of the KM-GBF, including: Persistent gender data gaps;Weak integration of gender equality into biodiversity monitoring systems;Limited methodological guidance for sex-disaggregated data collection;Institutional blind spots in national biodiversity reporting;Under-recognition of Indigenous women’s knowledge systems.By strengthening monitoring frameworks, this commitment contributes to credible and equitable global review of KM-GBF implementation.

GBF-INDICATOR-193; GBF-INDICATOR-21.CT.2
Strengthening women’s leadership, negotiation capacity and meaningful participation in the implementation and monitoring of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework

(a) Overview of scope and actions proposedThe CBD Women’s Caucus commits to systematically strengthening the leadership, negotiation capacity, technical expertise and meaningful participation of women and girls in all their diversity, including from Indigenous Peoples, and from local and Afro-descendant communities, in the implementation and monitoring of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KM-GBF) and the Gender Plan of Action (2023–2030). As the CBD major group representing women and girls, and a self-organised global network of over 800 members and 44 organisations across all regions, the CBD Women’s Caucus provides a structured platform to advance gender equality and human rights-based approaches within biodiversity governance. Rationale Despite recognition of women’s essential roles in biodiversity conservation and sustainable use, structural barriers continue to limit women’s participation in decision-making processes regarding international, regional and national negotiations on biodiversity conservation and sustainable, including because of: Unequal access to technical information and negotiation processes;Financial barriers to attending CBD meetings;Underrepresentation in delegations and expert bodies;Limited mentorship and leadership development opportunities;Language and knowledge barriers in highly technical negotiations.Addressing these structural barriers is a prerequisite for inclusive decision making, in particular, and for the effective and equitable implementation of the KM-GBF. Scope and actions1. Capacity development and negotiation preparednessSince 2023, the Caucus has implemented systematic preparatory processes for major CBD meetings, including: Virtual technical briefings on agenda items;Thematic webinars on monitoring the KM-GBF, resource mobilization, ecosystem restoration, digital sequence information and the Gender Plan of Action;Internal “Advancing our capacities and position” calls to deepen technical understanding;Development of negotiation guides and policy briefs;Multilingual training sessions (English, French, Spanish and additional languages where possible).These preparatory processes have been conducted prior to SBSTTA-24, SBSTTA-25, SBSTTA-26, SBI-4, SBI-5, COP16 (2024), COP16.2, SBSTTA-27 and SB8j-01(2025). 2. In-session coordination and peer support During CBD sessions, the Caucus provides: Daily coordination meetings;Joint analysis of draft decision texts;Collective position-building;Drafting support for interventions;Peer-to-peer mentoring and real-time negotiation guidance;Bilateral engagement coordination with Parties and regional groups.These efforts ensure that representatives speak from collectively agreed positions grounded in community realities and lived experience. 3. Leadership development and mentorship The Caucus supports emerging women leaders through: Mentorship from experienced negotiators;Simulation exercises and strategy sessions;Confidence-building support for delivering plenary interventions;Facilitation of speaking opportunities during side events and formal sessions.This contributes to sustained leadership development beyond individual meetings. 4. Participation in expert and advisory bodies Through a transparent internal nomination process prioritizing women from territories, Indigenous Peoples and local communities, the Caucus has supported participation in: Informal Advisory Group on Technical and Scientific Cooperation;Advisory Committee on Resource Mobilization;Technical Expert Group on Financial Reporting;Ad Hoc Technical Expert Group on Synthetic Biology;Informal Advisory Group on Benefit-sharing from Digital Sequence Information;Sustainable Ocean Initiative workshops;Ecosystem restoration expert processes;Advisory groups contributing to the Global Report on Collective Progress.This ensures that gender perspectives are embedded in highly technical discussions shaping KM-GBF implementation. 5. Dedicated platforms amplifying women’s leadership At COP16, the Women’s Pavilion and Women’s Forum created structured spaces for dialogue and political engagement, enabling Indigenous, grassroots and feminist leaders to: Share experiences;Advance rights-based approaches;Engage directly with negotiators and decision-makers;Strengthen alliances across sectors.These platforms contribute to visibility and sustained momentum for gender-responsive biodiversity governance. (b) Drivers and enabling conditions addressed This commitment addresses enabling conditions under Section C by responding to: Structural inequality in decision-making access;Underrepresentation of women in biodiversity governance spaces;Financial and logistical barriers to participation;Capacity gaps in negotiation and technical engagement;Limited institutional support for women’s leadership development.By strengthening inclusive governance and technical capacity, this commitment supports durable implementation of Target 23.
CGIAR
Italy
Isabel Lopez Noriega
i.lopez@cgiar.org
CGIAR genebanks and breeding programmes manage genetic resources in accordance with national and international ABS laws

CGIAR is a consortium of 15 independent agriculture research centers that work with over 3,000 partners worldwide to reduce poverty, increase food security, and improve natural resources in developing countries. Thirteen of the 15 Centers maintain international collections of genetic resources of plants, animals and microorganisms important for food and agriculture. Research and breeding for the development of improved crop varieties and animal breeds are at the core of CGIAR’s mandate. The acquisition, utilization and transfer of genetic resources and related information, including digital sequence information, are part of CGIAR scientists’ daily work. To ensure that they operate in accordance with national and international ABS laws, CGIAR puts at their disposal legal experts, training courses, guidelines and a helpdesk service. ABS due diligence and compliance is one of the CGIAR operative principles, as recognized in the CGIAR Principles on the Management of Intellectual Assets, among other CGIAR system-wide policies. In 1994, a high level policy oversight committee for CGIAR decided to establish a team of policy experts to assist CGIAR address issues related to genetic resources' policies, including access and benefit-sharing. While the personnel and the mode of delivery of ABS-related services have changed over the years, CGIAR has nonetheless maintained a small group of experts to provide such services since 1994. That team is currently embedded in the CGIAR Genebanks Accelerator.

Implementing integrated land use and management strategies through solution bundles that harmonize ecological integrity, socio-economic benefits, infrastructure development, and community requirements

Baseline biophysical and socioeconomic datasets are being systematically compiled and analysed for selected Living Landscapes in Africa, specifically Ethiopia and Kenya, to establish a comprehensive benchmarking reference for landscape condition, resource utilization patterns, production systems, and community livelihood dynamics. These benchmarks inform planning, monitoring, and adaptive management processes. Similarly, integrated Aquatic Resource and Land Management Planning initiatives in Kenya, Senegal, Tanzania, Tunisia, India, and Peru support the holistic optimization of terrestrial and aquatic resources across aquatic landscapes and seascapes. Through the development of targeted management strategies for rivers, reservoirs, ponds, wetlands, and associated agro-aquatic production systems, these efforts seek to improve water security, ecosystem health, fisheries productivity, and community livelihoods in Africa, Asia and the Americas.

Comité français de l'Union Internationale pour la Conservation de la Nature
BENJAMIN KONNERT
uicn@uicn.fr; benjamin.konnert@uicn.fr; sebastien.moncorps@uicn.fr
Fostering the widescale deployment of Nature-based Solutions and its Global Standard in France

IUCN defines Nature-based Solutions as “actions to protect, sustainably manage, and restore natural or modified ecosystems, that address societal challenges effectively and adaptively, simultaneously providing human wellbeing and biodiversity benefits” (IUCN, 2016). This definition was adopted at the 2016 IUCN World Conservation Congress with the Resolution 6.069 (WCC-2016-Res-069). In 2020, IUCN adopted its global Standard for Nature-based Solutions™ (NbS-GS), which was revised in 2025. The Standard provides a robust, evidence-based framework for the consistent and credible application of NbS across sectors and geographies. Structured as a systematic learning and quality assurance tool, the NbS-GS ensures that NbS are applied effectively and sustainably, maximizing their potential to address societal challenges, while delivering measurable benefits for people and nature. The French Committee of IUCN has supported the appropriation and deployment of NbS in France since its definition was formally adopted in 2016. NbS have been integrated in main public policies (national biodiversity strategy, climate plan…) and strategies and actions of various stakeholders like private companies, local authorities and NGOs. The French Committee of IUCN worked on NbS for different societal challenges (climate change, disaster risk reduction, and food security) through publications (analysis and presentations of case studies), information sharing, network building, and the development of a self-assessment tool based on the NbS-GS. In 2025, the Committee actively participated in the revision of the global standard for nature-based solutions. In 2026-2030, it will thrive to support the appropriation of the NbS-GS by all stakeholders and further demonstrate the role of NbS in solving contemporary societal challenges as water security in 2026 and socio-economic development in 2027. It will also run training sessions in France and abroad (Morocco, Tunisia etc.) and participate in national and international policy dialogues. In addition, the French Committee of IUCN will develop an awareness kit on NbS for the general public so that these solutions are better known to all and are systematically considered to meet the challenges of our societies.
GBF-T11. Nature’s contributions to people are restored, maintained and enhanced

Supporting the application of international standards on Protected and Conserved Areas in France

At the international level, IUCN works to establish practices, standards and policies that maximise the effectiveness of protected and conserved areas. As the official advisor on nature to the UNESCO World Heritage Committee, IUCN evaluates potential natural World Heritage sites and monitors those sites already recognised as the planet’s most important protected areas. The French Committee of IUCN supports the appropriation and application of international standards on Protected and Conserved Areas developed by IUCN in France by : Promoting the IUCN Green List of Protected and Conserved Areas in France, including by supporting new applications and existing labelled sites, to strengthen management effectiveness of protected areas in France;Fostering a greater understanding, appropriation and application of IUCN Protected Areas Management categories;Participating in the World Heritage sites applications review process;Identifying areas that met the criteria of Other Effective area-based Conservation Measures (OECM) in France.The French Committee of IUCN also actively promotes emerging approaches in conservation, such as “free evolving areas” that builds on passive rewilding approaches. In 2026-2030, the French Committee will seek to: Support the 25 Green List labelled sites and new sites in their applications to support the effective management of protected areas in France ; Develop a national methodology for the application of IUCN categories and guidelines to protected areas in France ;Support actual sites and new World Heritage sites applications ;Support the recognition and development of OECM in France ;Promote “free evolving areas” in line with resolution WCC-2025-Res-091 adopted at IUCN’s World Conservation Congress in 2025.
Identifying threatened species in France to better protect them

Established in 1964, the International Union for Conservation of Nature’s Red List of Threatened Species has evolved to become the world’s most comprehensive information source on the global extinction risk status of animal, fungus and plant species. Since 2006, the IUCN French Committee develops the Red List of Threatened Species in France in partnership with PatriNat (OFB-MNHN-CNRS-IRD): 17 367 species have been assessed and the data shows that 2 903 of them (16.7%) are threatened nationally (December 2024). These results : Identify threatened species that need conservation and restoration actionsSupport the revision of protected species’ list at the national levelContribute to environmental impact studiesFeed national observatories and inventories Shape conservation policies and action plansIn 2026-2030, the IUCN French Committee seeks to intensify its action for threatened species in France by: Coordinating the elaboration or actualisation of new Red List chapters on various species: reptiles and amphibians; sharks and rays; butterflies; coastal fish of the Antilles; birds; fungus; dragonflies and damselflies; fauna of La Réunion; mammals; lichens etc.Influencing policymaking and regulation to: ensure a better alignment between the conservation status of species and their protection at the national level. In 2024, an analysis from the IUCN French Committee showed that 56% of threatened species are not legally protected in France; support the fight against illegal wildlife trade by working with relevant authorities and stakeholders to implement specific actions and policies based on its 25 recommendations published in 2022 and national and international networks.

GBF-T05. Use, harvesting and trade of wild species is sustainable, safe and legal; GBF-T09. Management of wild species is sustainable and benefits people; GBF-T21. Data, information and knowledge for decision-making is available
Strengthening capacity building and policy for the prevention and management of Invasive Alien Species across sectors

Since 2017, the French Committee of IUCN coordinates the Centre de ressources Espèces Exotiques Envahissantes (CDR-EEE) or Resources Centre on Invasive Alien Species (IAS). The CDR-EEE provides knowledge and technical advice (methodologies, training, good practices) to improve IAS management for all the stakeholders : natural areas’ managers, NGOs, researchers, local governments, companies, public agencies and authorities. The French Committee of IUCN also coordinates the Réseau Espèces Exotiques Envahissantes Outre-Mer (REEE-OM) or Overseas Territories Network on Invasive Alien Species, where IAS is a main threat for biodiversity. In 2026-2030, the French Committee of IUCN will thrive to consolidate the activities of the CDR-EEE and REEE-OM via new knowledge products, training programs and new partnerships: Further support capacity building across all stakeholders, including by completing the existing 135 management case studies on nearly 60 IAS available on the CDR-EEE website;Contribute to shape conservation policy and action, including in the French Overseas Territories where recent research by the REEE-OM showed the impact of IAS on extinct, threatened and near-threatened species and where it could therefore help define local management and conservation priorities;Supporting the roll out and appropriation of IUCN’s Environmental Impact Classification of Alien Taxa (EICAT) for certain biological groups to identify and hierarchize IAS and prioritise management actions.Forge new partnerships with high-impact stakeholders, such as large-scale infrastructure managers that play a key role in the prevention and management of IAS. In 2026, the French Committee of IUCN will work with the three seaports of Guadeloupe, Guyane and Martinique, to build their IAS strategy and support capacity building and public awarness.

Supporting businesses and local governments in reinforcing their biodiversity commitments and harnessing the potential of innovative financing mechanisms

Since 2007, the French Committee of IUCN has forged strong relationships and partnerships with businesses and local governments that seek to integrate biodiversity in their strategies, plans and technical capacities, aligned with the national biodiversity strategy and the Gobal biodiversity framework. In 2026-2030, the French Committee of IUCN will seek to: Consolidate and forge new partnerships with businesses and local governments in France to help them understand and contribute to the objectives of the KMGBF and the EU Nature Restoration Regulation;Support the appropriation and use of tools and approaches enabling to better identify impacts and prioritise actions;Foster the emergence and adoption of innovative and high-integrity funding mechanisms for biodiversity, in line with IUCN’s resolution WCC-2025-Rec-078 “Regulating financing mechanisms based on biodiversity certificates and credits and guarantee positive effects on nature” by all relevant stakeholders, including businesses and local governments;Support its Members and partners, especially businesses in implementing Resolutions WCC-2025-Res-038 “Harnessing IUCN's power: Driving synergy for a net zero, nature positive and just energy transition” ; WCC-2025-Res-080 “Supporting the economy transformation towards a regenerative economy” adopted at the 2025 World Conservation Congress;Support its Members and partners, especially local governments in implementing Resolution WCC-2025-Res-102 “Reinforcing and developing urban and peri-urban forests in line with the IUCN Global Standard for Nature-based Solutions” adopted at the 2025 World Conservation Congress
