Achieving a systemic and robust consideration of gender in North-South conservation programs

The French Committee of IUCN is committed to supporting civil society organisations (CSOs) for conservation in developing countries. Since 2007, it funded 300+ conservation projects in 40+ countries (Africa, Asia and South America), totalling over 25 million euros in funding. Also with capacity building activities, the IUCN French Committee helps organizations succeed in their management and policy advocacy. To this end, the IUCN French Committee provides financial, technical, organizational and strategic support. To further enhance the support provided to civil society organizations, the French Committee of IUCN develop a gender strategy for its international cooperation programme. In line with the priorities identified in the guidance notes and objectives set out in Target 23 of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KMGBF), this strategy should enable a systemic and robust consideration of gender in all its operations. It should furthermore help achieve greater value coherence and alignment amongst the stakeholders (donors, beneficiaries, project coordinators) and, eventually, further enhance the quality of the support provided by the French Committee of IUCN to civil society organizations. The strategy will be developed throughout 2026 and rolled out in the following years across the intervention areas of IUCN French Committee (Africa, Asia, South America). Its development involves wide-scale consultations of beneficiaries, donors, project coordinators, and French Committee of IUCN’s staff members.

GBF-T20. Capacity-building and development, technology transfer, and technical and scientific cooperation for implementation is strengthened; GBF-T22. Ensure participation, justice, and rights for indigenous peoples and local communities, women, youth persons with disabilities and environmental defenders
Mobilizing knowledge, implementing and supporting actions for the restoration of degraded ecosystems

Considering Target 2 of the KMGBF and the adoption of the European Union Nature Restoration Regulation (EU NRR), the French Committee of IUCN identified nature restoration as a structuring and cross-cutting issue for its activities for the years to come. In 2025-2026, it supported the European Regional Office of IUCN and the French Ministry for Ecological Transition in the design of the future French Nature Restoration Plan via the Technical Support Instrument (TSI) funded by the European Commission. The first version of the national restoration plan is expected to be submitted in September 2026. National plans are expected to set out how countries will achieve the EU NRR objectives, including the objective to set out measures to restore 20% of degraded habitats by 2030. In 2026-2030, the French Committee of IUCN will seek to support the design, implementation and continuous improvement of the French nature restoration plan by mobilizing the best available knowledge and key networks in conservation: Consolidating the evidence base for nature restoration via the national Red list of threatened species and national Red list of ecosystems;Supporting decision-making and prioritisation of measures via the deployment of IUCN flagship tools in France (Nature-based solutions Global Standard ; identification of ecosystems or species at risk with the national Red lists) and other innovative tools such as CartNat to identify degraded ecosystems (Cartographie de la Naturalité);Providing technical advice and support to all stakeholders for the reduction of pressures on degraded ecosystems via the Resources Centre on Invasive Alien Species and Resources Centre on Tropical Wetlands in hexagonal and overseas territories;Supporting local governments and companies in the integration of nature restoration objectives in their existing planification strategies and tools. The French Committee of IUCN will also support restoration efforts in the French overseas territories mainly located in Biodiversity hotspots by : supporting the restoration actions for tropical wetlands, especially mangroves, included in the overseas chapter of the “national plan for wetlands” that it drafted following a large-scale consultation process and analysis (published in February 2026)pursuing its capacity building and training programs aimed at facilitating the restoration of biodiversity in agricultural ecosystems, with practices such as agroforestry in Mayotte implementing Nature-based Solutions in French Polynesia for coastal restoration (Fa’atura te Tahatai project) and supporting projects in French territories in the Pacific (BestLIFE 2030 programme).At the international level, the French Committee of IUCN will also contribute to achieving this objective by funding restoration projects in Africa through its “Programme de Petites Initiatives (PPI)”. Accordingly, four projects will each receive €75,000 in funding for the benefit of local conservation organizations based in Africa.

Supporting civil society action for conservation in developing countries

The French Committee of IUCN is committed to supporting civil society organisations (CSO) for conservation in developing countries. Since 2007, it funded 300+ conservation projects in 40+ countries (Africa, Asia and South America), totalling over 25 million euros in funding that improved nature conservation and local development. Also with capacity builiding activities, the IUCN French Committee helps organizations succeed in their management and policy advocacy. To this end, the IUCN French Committee provides financial, technical, organizational and strategic support. It has two flagship initiatives that aim empower civil society organizations (CSO) and strengthen their capacity and impacts: Programme de Petites Initiatives (PPI) (Small Scale Initiatives Programme) dedicated to CSOs in sub-Saharan Africa. Launched in 2006, it supported nearly 330 projects of 214 CSOs in 28 countries of Central and West Africa, with over 20M€ in funding;ProBioDev, dedicated to partnerships between CSOs in France and developing countries across the world (Africa, Asia, South America). Launched in 2024, the first phase of the ProbioDev program supports 11 projects implemented by 23 CSOs with 2,3 M€ in funding and capacity building.In 2026-2030, the French Committee of IUCN will: Deliver the 7th phase of the Programme de Petites Initiatives (PPI) ; Deliver the 1st and 2nd phases of ProBioDev ;Support PPI and ProBioDev OSCs and partners in their engagement with international conservation policy, e.g. World Conservation Congress, World Protected and Conserved Areas Congress, UNCBD COPs etc.

Lars Scharfe
vicepresident@europeanrangers.org

ERF advancing Target 3 of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework - Connecting Europe’s Rangers to empower them in their work for Planetary Health and making them heard as the Voice of Nature

Ranger’s Contribution to Target 3 Kunming-Montreal Target 3 of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework commits parties to ensuring that, by 2030, at least 30 per cent of terrestrial, inland water, coastal and marine areas are effectively conserved and managed. Designation alone does not deliver biodiversity outcomes. Effective implementation requires a sustained field presence, professional management, enforcement capacity, participatory governance, and long-term monitoring systems. Rangers play a crucial role in the effective management of protected areas. They translate the policy commitments of the Global Biodiversity Framework into tangible conservation results. Rangers are mediators between nature and humans. They give nature a voice and advertise as experts in nature conservation for a sustainable future. 1. Ensuring Protected Areas Effective Management and EnforcementRangers ensure compliance with conservation regulations, tackle illegal activities such as wildlife crime and habitat degradation, and assist with adaptive management planning. Their daily presence is essential to ensuring the effective management of protected areas, as required by the Global Biodiversity Framework's monitoring framework. 2. Delivering Monitoring, Reporting and Adaptive ManagementThe critical primary data provided by rangers through field-based biodiversity monitoring is essential for national reporting processes, including National Biodiversity Strategies and Action Plans (NBSAPs) and Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) reporting obligations. This data supports adaptive management and strengthens evidence-based decision-making. 3. Strengthening Ecological ConnectivityBy operating across protected areas, buffer zones and ecological corridors, rangers contribute to landscape-scale coherence and functional ecological networks, thus advancing the well-connected systems envisaged in Target 3. 4. Advancing Equity and Participatory GovernanceRangers facilitate dialogue with local communities, land users and stakeholders. They promote environmental education and conflict mediation, as well as inclusive governance approaches. This ensures that conservation measures are socially legitimate and equitably implemented. 5. Contributing to Climate Resilience and Ecosystem RestorationRangers implement and monitor ecosystem restoration activities and ecosystem-based adaptation measures, enhancing the resilience of protected and conserved areas in the face of climate change and increasing land-use pressures. Strategic Gaps and Structural ChallengesHowever, the relevant authorities are still unaware of the central role of rangers. According to Appleton et al. (2022), 1.5 million more rangers are needed worldwide to achieve the 30 by 30 goal. Ranger services across Europe are facing structural underinvestment, insufficient staffing levels, inconsistent professional recognition, and an increase in operational risks. Without proportional investment in ranger capacity, expanding spatial coverage risks creating paper parks rather than effectively conserved areas.
ERF: Networking and Capacity Building for Rangers to empower them and unite their voices to raise awareness of their key role It is important to bring Europe’s rangers and their diverse, highly specialised skills together to empower them for all aspects of their work. In line with the Sustainable Development Goals, this work ultimately leads to winning people over for sustainable action, integrating them into the protection of life on land & under water and helping to reduce inequalities by preserving natural resources. As the European Ranger Federation (ERF), we address these issues in Europe and beyond. We are bundling the voices of rangers into one strong voice for nature, making it heard in international decisions that impact nature. We also aim to create a stable basis for professional exchange by establishing a sustainable structure for it. This will contain a programme of networking and training, as well as strengthening the work of national ranger organisations and networks to improve working conditions, especially for female rangers. Important strategy steps: Advocate for Rangers as a bridge between local communities and nature conservation.Ranger Welfare: Ensuring that rangers receive the safety, appropriate equipment, professional training, reliable infrastructure and psychological support they deserve.Promote equity and inclusion by closing the gender and social-cultural gaps in the ranger profession.Elevating the Ranger Profession: the Ranger profession must be officially recognised.Establishing comparable professional standards across Europe.Fostering Effective Partnerships: Field-based partnerships between ranger associations, conservation organisations and local communities offer powerful models for success. These collaborations demonstrate how cross-sector cooperation can strengthen conservation outcomes, enhance professionalism and build human connections. Just as nature knows no borders, rangers should not work in isolation either. Conservation knows no borders: We are working to establish a dense network of protected areas with interconnected corridors (green infrastructure), and to achieve international recognition and coordinated action to safeguard these irreplaceable natural treasures.

Finland
Maija Rantamäki
maija.rantamaki@forestindustries.fi
Biodiversity Roadmap of the Finnish Wood Processing Industries

The roadmap reflects the wood processing industry’s commitment to safeguarding Finnish forest ecosystems, promoting nature-positive practices, and contributing to both national and international biodiversity goals. It invites forest owners, policymakers, NGOs, and research organisations to collaborate more closely. Based on an extensive research project, the roadmap examines the current state and future prospects of forest biodiversity, identifies key measures to strengthen it, and explores how technology can enhance biodiversity monitoring. The results show that many biodiversity indicators have improved in recent decades and that established forestry practices support this positive trajectory.
Finnish forest biodiversity is fostered through the protection of valuable habitats and forest management practices that support diverse species. Large-scale measures began in the 1990s, including reforms to the Forest Act and Nature Conservation Act, updated management guidelines, and the adoption of global forest certification schemes, all of which significantly expanded protected forest areas.
Nationwide inventories, have been crucial for tracking changes and assessing the effectiveness of biodiversity measures. Finland has a unique, over 100-year-old National Forest Inventory that has been developed since the 1980s to increasingly describe the changes taking place in structural features important for forest biodiversity. More over, extensive vegetation inventories, thus far carried out four times for forest species, provide information on changes in vegetation. This extensive dataset provides a comprehensive view of forest conditions and demonstrates that biodiversity has improved across many indicators.
Roughly half of Finland’s native species reside in forests, and evaluations of threatened species serve as key indicators of forest health. Forest operators also collect data during planning and harvesting, ensuring a robust knowledge base to evaluate impacts and guide ongoing development.
These findings form the foundation of the roadmap, which draws on decades of evidence to guide future actions. Actions proposed The biodiversity roadmap for the wood processing industry is based on the 5+5 principle (actions and enablers), which sets clear goals and direction for the coming years and decades. The five key actions are: • diversifying tree species • increasing the amount and quality of dead wood • safeguarding valuable habitats • enhancing the biodiversity of rare habitats • increasing fire habitats These are supported by five enablers: • increasing competence • strengthening cooperation • utilizing technology • emphasizing research and knowledge • reinforcing motivation The roadmap especially supports Target 10 of the Kunming-Montreal Agreement, which emphasizes the consideration and enhancement of biodiversity in production environments. In this way, the roadmap provides a concrete direction and tools for advancing forest biodiversity over the long term.

Kaveh Zahedi
Biodiversity@fao.org; OCB-Director@fao.org
FAO’s Strategy and Action Plan on Mainstreaming Biodiversity across Agricultural Sectors

The FAO Strategy on Mainstreaming Biodiversity across Agricultural Sectors was approved at the FAO Conference in 2019, in view of preparations for the post-2020 global biodiversity framework. The Strategy aims to mainstream biodiversity across agricultural sectors at national, regional and international levels in a structured and coherent manner, taking into account national priorities, needs, regulations and policies and country programming frameworks. The expected result of the application of the Strategy would be to reduce the negative impacts of agricultural practices on biodiversity, to promote sustainable agricultural practices and to conserve, enhance, preserve and restore biodiversity as a whole. The Action Plan for the implementation of the FAO Strategy on Mainstreaming Biodiversity Across Agricultural Sectors 2024–2027 reflects experiences gained from the implementation of the 2021–2023 Action Plan. It takes into account the Framework for Action on Biodiversity for Food and Agriculture, endorsed by the Commission on Genetic Resources for Food and Agriculture (CGRFA) and by the Council in 2021 and is aligned with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KMGBF). In alignment with the FAO Strategy on Mainstreaming Biodiversity across Agricultural Sectors, FAO implements the Action Plan based on the principles of effective governance, partnerships, a knowledge-based approach, inclusiveness, and gender equality and women’s empowerment.

Fundación Ambiente y Recursos Naturales
FARN
Ana Di Pangracio
adipangracio@farn.org.ar
“La Cuenca en la Escuela”: Environmental Education and Biocultural Awareness in the Matanza-Riachuelo Basin

The platform “La Cuenca en la Escuela” (The basin at School) is an online educational initiative developed by FARN to strengthen environmental education and biodiversity awareness among primary and secondary school teachers in the Matanza-Riachuelo Basin (MRB). This pedagogical resource provides methodological tools and interdisciplinary content for educators to integrate biodiversity, social justice, and intercultural perspectives into their teaching practices. The course fosters a deep understanding of the ecological and cultural complexity of the basin, encouraging the inclusion of local and traditional knowledge in the classroom. It is freely accessible online through FARN’s website, designed for autonomous use or integration into formal teacher training programs. The initiative strengthens local capacities for environmental stewardship and promotes responsible citizenship for biodiversity conservation. By promoting the integration of territorial knowledge and biocultural perspectives into formal education, La Cuenca en la Escuela supports the long-term objective of building an environmentally literate and participatory citizenry, aligned with both the NBSAP and the GBF. More information: https://farn.org.ar/la-cuenca-en-la-escuela/

Ecological restoration of the urban Santa Catalina Wetland for Biocultural Enhancement in the Matanza-Riachuelo Basin.

Since 2015, FARN, in collaboration with the Pilmayqueñ Foundation and the Museum of Agrobotany, History and Nature (MAgNa) of the Faculty of Agricultural Sciences at the National University of Lomas de Zamora (UNLZ), has been advancing the conservation and biocultural enhancement of the Santa Catalina Wetland, located in the southern metropolitan area of Greater Buenos Aires, Argentina, within the Matanza-Riachuelo Basin (MRB). This work builds upon more than twenty years of FARN’s experience in one of the most polluted basins in the world and responds to a 2008 Supreme Court ruling that ordered the restoration of the Basin to guarantee the human right to a healthy environment.
The project’s primary goal is to conserve and restore the biodiversity and cultural values of the Santa Catalina Wetland. The initiative aims to increase the area under active ecological restoration from 4.5 hectares to 6 hectares by 2026, with an interim target of 5 hectares by mid-2025. Actions include biota surveys, invasive alien species management, and the planting of more than 1,000 individuals from around 50 native species, supported by the local university nursery. Additional measures involve the construction of viewpoints, interpretative trails and educational signage, as well as archaeological explorations that highlight the long-standing human–nature relationship in the area. Biota surveys have led to the discovery of six species new to science, four species new for Buenos Aires province, and one plant species newly recorded as exotic for Argentina.
The initiative also strengthens the infrastructure and exhibitions of the local museum of nature and culture (MAgNa), turning it into a focal point for dialogue and public engagement on the status and heritage of Santa Catalina. Educational and biocultural restoration activities directly benefit approximately 2,000 students from schools and universities every year, including an average of 800 primary and secondary students, the broader university community (around 30,000 students on campus), and visitors participating in guided activities. Indirectly, approximately 800,000 inhabitants of surrounding districts (Lomas de Zamora, Esteban Echeverría, Almirante Brown, and La Matanza) benefit from the ecosystem services, environmental quality, recreational opportunities, and climate regulation provided by the wetland.
The commitment specifically addresses key direct and indirect drivers of biodiversity loss in a highly urbanised basin, including habitat degradation and fragmentation, water and soil pollution, invasive alien species, and loss of native vegetation along wetland margins. At the same time, it addresses underlying enabling conditions, including the weak implementation of protected area frameworks, unsustainable land-use dynamics, limited public access to green spaces, and low environmental awareness. By combining ecological restoration, community participation, education, and advocacy, the initiative helps reverse these drivers and strengthens the long-term conservation of the biocultural values in Santa Catalina.

Biocultural Corridor of the Matanza Riachuelo Basin

FARN, with other partner organisations in Argentina, promotes biocultural corridors as defined geographical spaces — urban, peri-urban, or rural — that safeguard natural and cultural heritage (both tangible and intangible), maintain healthy, interconnected ecosystems, and foster connections between existing and future protected areas. These corridors integrate restoration processes and promote socially and ecologically responsible productive and residential uses. Biocultural Corridors contribute to environmental territorial planning and seek to promote environmental recovery and restoration through a participatory, rights-based conservation model adapted to urban and peri-urban environments. The Matanza-Riachuelo Basin (MRB) biocultural corridor, in particular, is an initiative developed by FARN, building on its two decades of work in the basin. It connects key wetland areas across the basin — including Santa Catalina, Laguna de Rocha, and Ciudad Evita — fostering ecological restoration, community monitoring, and environmental education. It combines science, community participation, and advocacy to advance an integrated approach to biodiversity conservation, social inclusion, and environmental justice. The biocultural corridor concept has already been recognised by National Resolution 356/2022, which extended the National Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan period (NBSAP 2016–2020), as a possible Other Effective Area-based Conservation Measure (OECM). Draft laws on biocultural corridors have been submitted to the legislatures of Buenos Aires and Entre Ríos provinces. More information: https://farn.org.ar/corredor-biocultural-riachuelo/

GBF-INDICATOR-2.2; GBF-INDICATOR-3.1; GBF-INDICATOR-12.1
Protection of the Gulf of San Matías in Argentina

The Gulf of San Matías is located in northern Patagonia, Argentina, within a coastal–marine region of high ecological significance that includes and borders several protected and conserved areas at provincial and national levels. These include provincial coastal protected areas along the Río Negro shoreline (such as Caleta de los Loros–Pozo Salado–Punta Mejillón and the Bahía de San Antonio area), the recently created Islote Lobos National Park, and the broader biogeographic connection with the Valdés Peninsula, a globally recognised biodiversity hotspot for marine mammals, seabirds, and coastal ecosystems. These areas form part of a marine–coastal conservation mosaic that provides critical habitat for migratory species, breeding colonies of seabirds, marine mammals, and fish stocks, and delivers essential ecosystem services, including climate regulation, coastal protection, and food security. The Gulf of San Matías also functions as a key ecological corridor within the Argentine Sea. FARN’s commitment seeks to prevent the degradation of this highly sensitive marine environment due to the expansion of offshore oil and gas extraction, hydrocarbon transport infrastructure, and liquefied natural gas (LNG) export facilities. The initiative promotes the effective protection of existing protected areas, the prevention of incompatible activities in ecologically significant zones, and the application of the precautionary and ecosystem-based approaches in marine governance. Since January 2024, FARN has developed a comprehensive line of work aimed at preventing the transformation of the Gulf of San Matías into a sacrifice zone. This work combines legal analysis, public participation, strategic litigation, policy advocacy, and international incidence to ensure compliance with environmental law, biodiversity conservation obligations, and human rights standards. Key actions undertaken include: The production of technical and legal reports analysing the environmental, climatic, socio-economic, and governance impacts of offshore fossil fuel expansion in the Gulf of San Matías and the Argentine Sea. This includes: analysis of methane emissions associated with hydrocarbon infrastructure expansion in Argentina; documentation of socio-environmental risks and testimonies from potentially affected communities in the Gulf region; and financial analysis linking fossil fuel expansion projects to national and international financial actors.Active participation in public hearings related to LNG vessels, oil terminals, and hydrocarbon infrastructure, documenting procedural irregularities and restrictions to access to information and participation.The submission of legal actions and formal objections highlighting violations of environmental impact assessment standards, the precautionary principle, and the Escazú Agreement.Advocacy before national authorities and provincial governments to demand transparency, cumulative impact assessments, and ecosystem-based marine planning.International advocacy, including the promotion and approval of a motion at the IUCN Congress calling for the protection of the Gulf of San Matías and High-Andean wetlands, recognising their ecological value and vulnerability.Participation in the “Golfo Azul para Siempre” campaign, aimed at preventing the financing of offshore oil and gas and LNG infrastructure in the Gulf of San Matías. This includes direct advocacy towards national and international financial actors—providing evidence-based analysis on environmental, climate, and socio-economic impacts—and public communication strategies to raise awareness among broader audiences.Public communication and media engagement to raise awareness of biodiversity risks and socio-environmental conflicts associated with fossil fuel expansion.This commitment addresses several direct and indirect drivers of biodiversity loss, including: Direct drivers: Habitat degradation and fragmentation due to offshore hydrocarbon exploration and extraction; underwater noise pollution from seismic surveys; increased risk of oil spills; impacts on marine fauna, fisheries, and coastal ecosystems.Indirect drivers: Financial incentives and investment flows that enable fossil fuel expansion, contributing to biodiversity loss and climate impacts in marine ecosystems; weak enforcement of environmental regulations; lack of cumulative and strategic environmental assessments; restricted public participation; and governance frameworks prioritising extractivist development over biodiversity protection. Global Youth Biodiversity Network France
GYBN France
Youth organization
Marie-Morgane Rouyer
gybn.france@gmail.com
Supporting the meaningful participation of French youth in biodiversity governance and protection

GYBN France, created in 2023, is the French chapter of the Global Youth Biodiversity Network (GYBN), the Youth Constituency in the CBD. GYBN France is committed to improving youth participation in biodiversity governance in France. To this end, we provide feedback on biodiversity-related legislation in France and its assessments. We provided comments on the French NBSAP at the times of its drafting and revision, to government representatives and various stakeholders involved in its implementation. We are continually strengthening our efforts to ensure that our comments are better taken into account and that young people are better recognized as key actors in the implementation of our NBSAP. To this end, we sit on the National Biodiversity Committee, where we will continue to represent the voice of young people. We are particularly committed to working with key stakeholders in France's National Biodiversity Strategy to enable young people to get involved in implementing this Strategy. In particular, we have identified governance bodies where youth participation could be improved. We are also involved in important events related to biodiversity governance and conservation, such as CBD events (COP15,16, SBI, SBSTTA, expert workshops), capacity-building workshops (e.g., in connection with the COOP4CBD project, national workshops on NBSAPs), by linking up CBD work with other United Nations conventions (GYBN France member associations regularly participate in UNFCCC COPs), and at the United Nations Ocean Conference (UNOC). We coordinated part of the international and national youth participation in the previous UNOC held in France in June 2025. We are committed to continuing our efforts to raise awareness on biodiversity and its governance among young people, to drafting advocacy papers on biodiversity, to communicating on issues related to national and international biodiversity governance, and to creating collaborations between French youth associations and biodiversity stakeholders in France. We are committed to strengthening these collaborations so that young people, including those living in French overseas territories, have greater access and are better recognized as key stakeholders in biodiversity governance in France.
Which direct and indirect drivers or enabling conditions of biodiversity loss the commitment will seek to address We are working to improve the indirect causes of biodiversity loss, in particular by making institutional and governance structures fairer and more inclusive for younger generations and those to come. Initiating such transformative changes in our modes of governance is a necessary condition for halting biodiversity loss. This requires challenging values and a sociocultural context that fails to recognize and value young people as key actors of biodiversity decision-making. Through our advocacy work, we are also committed to challenging the direct causes of biodiversity loss.
GBF-INDICATOR-274
Global Youth Biodiversity Network Indonesia
GYBN Indonesia
Indonesia
Cita Ramadhanti
citardht@gmail.com; gybn.indonesia20@gmail.com
Strengthening Youth Capacity for Biodiversity Governance through YouBisa

Since Global Youth Biodiversity Network (GYBN) became a constituent of the Conference of the Parties (COP) to the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) in 2012, young people have been recognized not merely as observers, but as actors capable of contributing constructive perspectives and positive momentum for the future of biodiversity. Surveys conducted by GYBN Indonesia in 2024 and 2025 indicate that the engagement of young people in Indonesia is more complex and substantive than what is often portrayed on social media. Recognizing the continued active role of young people, the COP process provides an important momentum for documentation, reflection, and ensuring the continuity of meaningful youth participation and contributions. Through the Youth Biodiversity Squad (YouBisa) initiative, GYBN Indonesia creates a youth-centered, inclusive, reflective, and practice-oriented learning space. This initiative is designed as an entry point for young people with science and non-biodiversity-related educational backgrounds to better understand the systemic drivers of biodiversity loss, the mechanisms of the CBD and other MEAs, and the strategic role of youth in advancing policy change and concrete actions while providing intergenerational mentorship. Furthermore, this initiative was launched as part of efforts to contribute to the achievement of National Targets 16 and 17, as well as KM-GBF Target 20, 21, and 22, as outlined in the Indonesian Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan (IBSAP). YouBisa is an intensive one-month capacity-building program that will be structured around four interactive discursive sessions and three weeks of guided pedagogical engagement. The curriculum synthesizes global biodiversity governance frameworks; the Convention on Biological Diversity and the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework, with systems thinking and Theory U, a methodological framework for personal and collective transformation, complemented by in-depth thematic dialogues. Participant selection strategically prioritizes youth from geographically marginalized regions of the Indonesian archipelago. This deliberate inclusion aims to amplify youth voices, which are frequently structurally excluded from national-level discourse, thereby ensuring their substantive participation in biodiversity governance and decision-making processes. Through this intensive program, we aim to equip youth with empirical perspectives and a more nuanced comprehension of the socio-ecological dimensions of biodiversity. This foundational knowledge empowers them to conceptualize and operationalize initiatives that transcend conventional project-based models, embedding them instead deeply within local wisdom and systemic problem-solving approaches. Ultimately, this initiative represents a critical foundational step toward enabling safe, collaborative, and dialogic spaces for youth across Indonesia.

Making the invisible visible: UK Youth commitments and contributions to the GBF

GYBN UK’s mission is to empower, enable and unite youth across the UK, through building a coalition of young people and organisations across the four countries, inspiring all young people to be leaders for nature, particularly in policy and decision making processes. One of our core objectives is to make youth voices heard, which includes supporting the UK Government and sub-national bodies to have meaningful youth representation in NBSAPs and reporting processes. GYBN UK have collated delivered and committed actions from a total of 24 youth-led or focused organisations, groups, panels/ boards, including GYBN UK itself. These commitments not only provide details of actions delivered by young people in contribution to the UK NBSAP and GBF, but also provide insight and an indication of the under-reported work done by all young people across the UK and beyond. The following table outlines planned or already undertaken work by youth-led or focused organisations as per their submissions to GYBN UK. Against each of the UK National and GBF targets, we have listed the organisations which have declared their contribution to a given target to us. Where an organisation has detailed work against a specific target, we have in some instances included that written description, with some editing, in the table. Where an organisation has not given a written description against a specified target, but has stated that they are contributing towards it, their names are listed as ‘other contributions’ (this list also contains the names of organisations that have provided written descriptions which, at the discretion of the editors, were not included, e.g. if it was similar to another written description against another target). It has been taken in good faith that these organisations have been accurate in the reflections of their work and GYBN UK has not verified the accuracy of the evidence submitted to us. With the following work delivered either by young people and/or for young people, the primary national and global target is target 22. However, as demonstrated clearly by the table, the work of young people is relevant to nearly all 23 targets. For organisations whose remit/ work goes beyond the work led or delivered by young people, this has not been included. In these cases, it is therefore likely that they contribute to further targets that are not captured here. We have done our best to be clear where an organisation’s contribution is specifically attributed to their youth panel/board or a specific youth-focused programme they deliver. Similarly, given the nature of some of the listed organisations’ work, they also contribute to the national commitments of other countries. However, these elements are not included in this submission. One such example is Reserva: Youth Land Trust, which works in Ecuador and Colombia. The following organisations submitted contributions to GYBN UK and therefore feature in this report: 2050 Climate GroupAction for Conservation Ancient Woodlands UKBTO (British Trust for Ornithology) YouthChester Zoo Youth BoardCPRE (Council for the Preservation of Rural England)Dartmoor National Park Authority (NPA)Field Studies CouncilGlobal Youth Biodiversity Network UK (GYBN UK)Loch Lomond & The Trossachs National ParkLondon Wildlife TrustMammal SocietyNature Positive UniversitiesNew Forest Youth BoardNorth York Moors National Park Authority (NPA)Pembrokeshire Coast National Park Authority (PCNPA)Reserva: Youth Land TrustUK Man and the Biosphere Youth NetworkUK Youth for NatureUrban Wildling HubYorkshire Dales National Park Authority (NPA)Young Sea Changers ScotlandYoungWildersZSL (Zoological Society of London) This section provides an overview of the contributions of young people (including youth-led organisations, youth-coordinating organisations, and other organisations with a youth-voice or youth-focus) to the targets set out in the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework.Target 1: Plan and Manage all Areas To Reduce Biodiversity LossLondon Wildlife Trust Youth Board and Youth Work Promoting legislation to protect nature/wildlife: for example, our Youth Board took part in our recent online campaign to encourage Londoners to reach out to their MPs to support Amendment 40 to the Planning & Infrastructure Bill. Other Contributions: UK Youth 4 Nature, North York Moors National Park Authority (youth focused programmes) Target 2: Restore 30% of all Degraded EcosystemsYoungwilders CIC We work with landowners and councils to rewild and restore degraded land, from meadow creation in central London to woodland restoration in Northumberland. Our projects involve young people in surveying species, removing invasive species such as Sitka spruce and Giant Hogweed, and improving habitats for pollinators and native plants. In 2024–25, Youngwilders ran 12 youth-led nature recovery projects across nine counties, restoring 210+ acres through meadows, wetlands, hedgerows, and grazing schemes. Over 400 young people took part, 35% from underrepresented backgrounds, gaining hands-on skills through programmes like Wild Stewards and Wayfinders. UK Youth For Nature UKY4N works with partners who are actively restoring and protecting nature, to raise awareness of core biodiversity issues as well as driving attention to those who are restoring it. Our work across farming and chemicals works to restore nature at all levels to ensure a more nature-rich future for all. Action for Conservation Our Intergenerational Nature Restoration Projects restore and protect nature, and contribute to climate mitigation across 665 acres in South Wales, Derbyshire and Bristol, in partnership with the Penpont Estate, Avon Wildlife Trust and Heartwood Social Farm. Young people, alongside their communities, have delivered hundreds of restoration actions to improve, restore and protect nature in line with community-developed visions for the land. At our flagship Penpont Project, we have planted 20,000+ trees, created a new community tree nursery, restored wildflower meadows and wetlands, carried out extensive freshwater and riparian restoration and are shifting to regenerative grazing, agro-forestry and continuous-cover forestry regimes. London Wildlife Trust Youth Board and Youth Work Trainees and Youth Board members support the management of 36 nature reserves across London, ranging from woodland to meadow, wetlands. Support in the planning and practical delivery of the reintroduction of species in London: for example, glow worms in Croydon and beavers & white storks in Barking and Dagenham. Activities that feed into restoration degraded habitats include; woodland management and restoration, visitor access, farm management, species surveys, INNS removal, peatland restoration and more. 2025 saw 53 young people participate in DYR. North York Moors National Park Authority (youth focused programmes) Young people actively contribute to nature restoration through hands-on habitat management, including heathland, woodland and wetland restoration, improving ecosystem condition across the National Park. Mammal Society Youth-led habitat restoration: Young volunteers participate in local activities led by local mammal groups, directly contributing to the restoration of local ecosystems. CPRE This is the primary aim of Hedgerow Heroes. We are actively hedge planting, laying, coppicing and gapping up hedgerows and teaching people these skills. In Hampshire we have funded hedgelaying apprenticeships for several young people and in Shropshire, planting hedgerows with local scouts has been valuable. Our Hedgerow Heroes project has been going for four years with the aim of planting and restoring hedgerows across England. We are currently collaborating with Young Wilders planting/restoring hedgerows with schools, colleges and youth organisations. Last year (2024-5) Hedgerow Heroes engaged 2489 children and 298 young people (18-30yrs) and 22 schools and colleges. Young volunteers also support the management of the Park including maintaining paths and trails, planting trees and removing litter. Much of the programme focus is on improving education and access to relevant and accurate information on sustainable consumption, helping people reduce the global footprint of consumption in an equitable manner. New Forest Youth Board Working with local green community groups to encourage sustainable practices such as attending repair cafes, volunteering at a community allotment, and clearing up after themselves and their pets when out in nature (social media workshop).Contributed to the organisation of and spoke at local and regional Transport Summits on public transport usage. Yorkshire Dales National Park Authority (youth focused programmes) Education/school groups include elements of climate change by learning about what it is, what sustainability is, how it impacts our world, and what we can do to help the environment. E.g., exploring peat, changing habitats. This fosters early inspiration and understanding of the issues to young people with practical tools. Learning skills for sustainable consumption choices, such as repairing and mending, also included in the USDD programme. North York Moors National Park Authority (youth focused programmes) Explorers and Young Rangers promote sustainable behaviours by encouraging responsible access to nature and increasing understanding of human impacts on ecosystems. Nature Positive Universities (Student Ambassadors) Nature Positive Student Ambassadors promote initiatives at their institutions focused on sustainable food as well as waste and resource use. This has included for example, reusable cup schemes, water refill stations, recycling and awareness campaigns, clothing swap shops, growing vegetables on campus and repair cafes. Chester Zoo (Youth Board) The Youth Board working groups support the sustainable consumption policies of the wider Zoo including the Sustainable Palm oil and deforestation-free commodities work. ZSL Youth Advisory Board Workshops for the National Saturday Club focused on developing participants' understanding of what actions they can take to benefit nature including environmentally sustainable behaviours. Other contributions from: Dartmoor National Park Authority (DNPA) (youth focused programmes), Pembrokeshire Coast National Park Authority (PCNPA) Next Generation, Field Studies Council Target 17: Strengthen Biosafety and Distribute the Benefits of BiotechnologyN/ATarget 18: Reduce Harmful Incentives by at Least $500 Billion per Year, and Scale Up Positive Incentives for BiodiversityN/ATarget 19: Mobilize $200 Billion per Year for Biodiversity From all Sources, Including $30 Billion Through International FinanceReserva: The Youth Land Trust We have conducted two in-person workshops and two virtual workshops to train UK youth in fundraising techniques to help them mobilize private sector funding for conservation actions. The funds we raise go directly to conservation projects in the tropics. Target 20: Strengthen Capacity-Building, Technology Transfer, and Scientific and Technical Cooperation for BiodiversityGYBN UK One of our objectives is to build capacity by improving biodiversity policy literacy amongst youth, and facilitating and supporting their participation in decision-making processes for national, European and international biodiversity policies. This includes delivering social media campaigns and workshops demystifying complex international and national biodiversity policy spaces such as the CBD and IPBES, and national policy decisions and papers. Training and peer-to-peer learning is provided to those who attend events and conferences to ensure they can meaningfully participate in and influence the decisions made there. UK Youth For Nature UKY4N works with partners across the environmental sector, reaching into the health and social space too. We work to bring youth voices together in the nature space, doing so enables young people to learn from experts as well as share common ground on issues they are concerned about within the biodiversity arena. We also collaborate with government and decision making bodies to advise on youth participation. Youngwilders CIC Every project is co-led by young people aged 18–30, supported through paid training, mentoring, and ecological skill-building. Our Wayfinders field skills sessions and national Overgrowth gatherings build a community of early-career environmentalists, promoting confidence, collaboration, and long-term engagement in the sector. 2050 Climate Group Since 2014, we have been working to empower Scotland’s Young Leaders to lead action on tackling our climate crisis including through capacity building. We are a youth-led and volunteer-driven organisation with over 40 volunteers based across Scotland. Our Climate Action Leadership Programme equips young people with the leadership skills and climate change knowledge needed to take effective action. The Leaders Network looks to provide information in an engaging and accessible manner including film screenings, events and panels, and hosting Scotland’s Youth Climate Summit. Our policy work looks to support Young Leaders in taking action on the policy issues they want to address on all levels of governance including running workshops and events to facilitate understanding of policy issues and processes. Mammal Society Students participating in mammal related research can present their findings at the Society’s Annual Conference, giving young researchers a platform to share insights, engage with professionals, and influence conservation action.Youth Ambassadors and UMAC volunteers create social media content, school resources, and outreach materials to share knowledge about mammals and biodiversity with peers and local communities. ZSL Youth Advisory Board Members run workshops including upskilling participants on their Citizen Science Skills at the 2025 Chester Youth Symposium, alongside collecting perspectives on how to get more young people involved in citizen science monitoring programmes. BTO Youth Free training for youth volunteers – this includes a wide range of subjects, such as bird identification, statistics and acoustic monitoring. This helps them to improve their knowledge base on various biological areas, from monitoring to behaviour. Many Youth Rep events focus on teaching identification or survey techniques to young people. Equipment Donation scheme – we redistribute birdwatching equipment to young people under 21 (groups and individuals) who cannot afford their own, again removing the barrier of cost to youth participation in biodiversity. Reserva: The Youth Land Trust We lead capacity building workshops for UK youth on other topics, including Rights of Nature, climate change communication strategy, science communication, animation, and research project design. Yorkshire Dales National Park Authority (youth focused programmes) Each Up Skill, Down Dale (USDD) session is focused on capacity-building through skill-building, raising awareness, and creating an environment where young people can ask questions and get involved with staff, volunteers, and each other. Roots to Jobs (RTJ) is focused around introducing new skills and knowledge in the outdoors through the most accessible ways with people knowing little English. Dartmoor National Park Authority (DNPA) (youth focused programmes) Girls Do Dartmoor (GDD): Participants in the GDD programmes are girls who are likely to struggle with anxiety, low confidence or self-esteem, and/or do not engage with physical activity. They take part in a wide range of activities that help them discover and learn about their local protected landscape, enjoy and build a connection with it, and receive the contribution that nature has on personal wellbeing. 41 girls took part in 2025. London Wildlife Trust Youth Board and Youth Work Our paid traineeship programme teaches young people the skills and knowledge needed for a green career. The Keeping it Wild Collective engages young people aged 16-30 with nature through masterclasses, small-scale volunteering and creative activities, helping them to develop green skills and leadership capabilities. Sessions are hosted by young people who are paid for their time and skills. Our primary school engagement brings over 10,000 students per year to our sites to encourage them to engage with and learn about nature and biodiversity. North York Moors National Park Authority (youth focused programmes) Building young people’s capacity to engage with biodiversity by developing confidence, ecological understanding, practical conservation skills, leadership, and the ability to participate in decision-making and advocacy. Field Studies Council The Field Studies Council is a UK-based environmental education charity which prioritises equal access to nature education and improved nature engagement. In 2025 we educated over 110,00 school students on geography and biology, and over 6,300 adults on biodiversity with courses ranging from biodiversity literacy and awareness to species identification and surveying. The Young Darwin Scholarship hosts dozens of young budding naturalists every year, enabling them to meet like-minded people and expand on their interests in the natural environment. Nature Positive Universities (Student Ambassadors) Nature Positive Student Ambassadors conduct and report on on-campus activities aimed at discussing and raising awareness on topics such as biodiversity, sustainable choices and climate change. This included creating social media accounts, using photography and digital communications, organising sessions and competitions, launching campaigns, and creating new student societies. Chester Zoo (Youth Board) The Youth Board contributes to capacity-building projects including hosting the Chester Zoo Youth Summit which develops knowledge, skills, network connections and attitudes of attendees. Youth Board members have also presented at events such as the BIAZA Research Conference 2025, which supported biodiversity governance, cross-sectoral coordination and collaboration, and strengthening the youth voice in governance. Ancient Woodlands UK Capacity building events and communications on several topics including why woodlands are important for young people and platforming young voices, raising awareness of ancient woodlands and increasing their support, discussing climate change mitigation by ancient woodlands, raising awareness of invasive species and advocating for their removal, working towards legal protection for ancient woodlands and pushing for their restoration, and increasing participation from women in conservation. Urban Wilding Hub Through the Wilding Partners programme we run monthly knowledge sharing events about urban wilding. We currently have 33 members from 8 European countries with most members in the UK. We run public webinars to share urban wilding principles and case studies with a wider audience. Other contributions from: UK Man and the Biosphere Youth Network, Loch Lomond & The Trossachs National Park Authority (Youth Committee and programmes), Pembrokeshire Coast National Park Authority (PCNPA) Next Generation, Young Sea Changers Scotland, New Forest Youth Board, Target 21: Ensure That Knowledge Is Available and Accessible To Guide Biodiversity ActionUK Youth For Nature UKY4N trains and creates opportunities for young people to share their own research as well as be a voice for sharing knowledge on biodiversity across the UK, attending events, delivering facilitated workshops and panels on nature and climate issues here in the UK. UK Man and the Biosphere Youth Network The network connects young people from the UK’s 7 UNESCO Biosphere Reserves to encourage collaboration and information sharing, as well as allowing for meaningful representation of young people in these designated sites at a higher level. Since then the network has worked together to run a coordinated bioblitz for youth in the UK, Ireland and Crown Dependencies Biosphere Reserves. This included teaching on the value of biosphere reserves and the other locations around the British Isles. Young Sea Changers Scotland YSCS equips young people with the knowledge, skills and confidence to understand and engage with marine policy, empowering them to participate meaningfully in decision-making. Through workshops, webinars, toolkits, and social media, YSCS translates complex policy issues, processes, and jargon into accessible, youth-friendly formats, facilitating access to information. ZSL Youth Advisory Board Members have developed talks, zoo-based workshops and online content aimed at uniting diverse groups of young people as advocates for nature. Through activities for our science department’s summer school, workshops for the National Saturday Club, videos for Global Actions Plan’s Earth Day celebrations and participation in the Children’s Rights Enquiry in Parliament, the board has engaged over 1,025 young people with their work. New Forest Youth Board We have shared biodiversity knowledge and engaged with the topic at events such as the New Forest show, Thrive Festival, Urban Wild on the Common, and at a Forestry England Bioblitz, where our audience is locals and visitors, families and young people. We are working to promote key messages to desired audiences through social media, posters/signage and event engagement. BTO Youth BTO Youth looks to improve accessibility of biodiversity information through; the Youth in Nature Summits – specific events focussed on helping attendees to learn more about birds, nature and conservation; free education resources - our Birding 101 series was aimed at sharing knowledge about birds, ranging from topics such as migration to waterbird ID, with young people; and Free Bird Camps - each year we run about 5 bird camps for teenagers, which are events where youth volunteers and staff can share knowledge with participants, and participants are also actively encouraged to share their knowledge with each other. Reserva: The Youth Land Trust Our members publish work in scientific journals, including UK-based team members who have participated or led the description of new species to science. We also share knowledge about biodiversity conservation on our social media and through other forms of media, including long form documentary, educational workshop, and public lecture. Yorkshire Dales National Park Authority (youth focused programmes) Our education groups link to the national curriculum through hands-on work – e.g. exploring habitats, understanding peat, wildlife monitoring, river surveys. This can include working with other teams in the National Park Authority, like the Wildlife team or the Heritage team to share current and important information on local areas. Field Studies Council Field Studies Council is a leading provider of biodiversity training delivered in-person in the UK and online to an increasingly global audience. The Youth Panel reviews Field Studies Council biodiversity training products, provides in-depth feedback and devises innovative promotional approaches to engage new audiences with biodiversity knowledge and training. North York Moors National Park Authority Supporting biodiversity knowledge-sharing by engaging young people at different stages. From introduction, hands-on learning, ecological understanding, practical conservation/monitoring, how biodiversity knowledge is used in policy, planning and management decisions. Chester Zoo (Youth Board) The Chester Zoo Youth Board Conservation Education and Engagement working group advises on the Zoos work to share knowledge on biodiversity through education clubs, courses, events, webinars, digital lessons, and social media content.Sharing of biodiversity knowledge is also conducted through the annual Chester Zoo Youth Summit. Other contributions from: 2050 Climate Group, Youngwilders CIC, Dartmoor National Park Authority (DNPA) (youth focused programmes), Loch Lomond & The Trossachs National Park Authority (Youth Committee and programmes), Pembrokeshire Coast National Park Authority (PCNPA) Next Generation, London Wildlife Trust Youth Board and Youth Work, Nature Positive Universities (Student Ambassadors), Urban Wilding Hub, Mammal Society Target 22: Ensure Participation in Decision-Making and Access to Justice and Information Related to Biodiversity for allGYBN UK We are an entirely youth-led (18-35 years) group with a mission to empower, enable and unite youth across the UK, through building a coalition of young people and organisations across the four countries, inspiring all young people to be leaders for nature, particularly in policy and decision making processes. We work with UK and devolved governments to provide a route for young people to feed into and influence policy and decision-making. Example mechanisms include; annual representation at JNCC Committee meetings, representation on Scotland's National Biodiversity Forum for the NBSAP, attendance of UK Government stakeholder forums for UNEA, IPBES, CBD and Environment Improvement Plan, responding to consultations on behalf of young people using the UK Youth Priorities for Nature. UK Youth For Nature UKY4N brings young people to the table with decision makers and politicians to ensure that youth voices - including under represented groups - are represented in decision making regarding the environment and nature. We train young people in areas such as networking and public speaking. Ancient Woodlands UK A youth-led campaign, looks to engage people in the protection (legal) and conservation of ancient woodland habitats in the UK providing information and pathways for people, including youth, to participate. This includes working with partners to draft a legal bill for the full protection of ancient woodlands and exploring a rights-of-nature approach. 2050 Climate Group 2050 Climate Group represents and engages in the policy development process on behalf of young people, responding to environmental policy consultations and representing the youth voice in a number of decision-making/policy-making spaces including consultation panels such as that for single-use plastic in Scotland. Reserva: The Youth Land Trust Reserva focuses on building creative pathways for youth from all backgrounds to participate in the conservation of earth’s most biodiverse and threatened areas. The non-profit has a majority membership of UK youth, including 45 UK youth members on our Youth Council and 40% UK members on our Board of Directors. Our mission is to empower young people around the world to make a measurable difference for threatened species and habitats through conservation, education, and storytelling. Primarily, this centres around youth-led efforts to create, support, explore, and defend protected areas in biodiversity hotspots in close collaboration with local partners. Young Sea Changers Scotland YSCS represents young people’s perspectives in various policy spaces and ensures that diverse youth voices actively shape marine policy priorities. By advocating for meaningful youth participation in policymaking, YSCS works to institutionalise youth participation as a key mechanism for fair, forward-looking marine governance. Action for Conservation We deliver education, leadership and nature restoration programmes for young people from diverse backgrounds in the UK. Since 2014, we have supported 7,000+ young people to design and deliver environmental projects and campaigns that benefit biodiversity and people in their communities. We use unique eco-cultural mapping methods to effectively involve young people from diverse backgrounds in land-based decision-making at our Intergenerational Nature Restoration Projects, addressing a longstanding need in the nature sector. This work has been cited as the “gold standard of co-design” by others in the sector. Mammal Society The Youth Network empowers 18–30-year-olds to lead working groups, co-design projects, and host webinars and community events, fostering leadership and collaboration among young conservationists. In 2025 we convened a working group of young people to guide how youth engagement occurs within the State of Nature Partnership, ensuring that young voices shape national biodiversity reporting and policy discussions. Youth ambassadors actively participate in policy working groups to help shape education policy to include more time in nature. Most recently we wrote an open letter on the decline of fieldwork in life science degree programs. Chester Zoo (Youth Board) Chester Zoo Youth Board is comprised of a diverse group of young people, from a variety of backgrounds, representing the voice of underrepresented groups to ensure the conservation and biodiversity work sector is accessible and inclusive. The youth board has contributed to Chester Zoo’s Inclusive Growth Strategy. ZSL Youth Advisory Board The youth board itself is a mechanism for youth participation in conservation, working alongside ZSL’s board of directors to ensure the youth voice is incorporated into decision making at all levels. Members have run multiple workshops and participated in panels on the importance of youth participation to over 115 attendees at youth summits in Cambridge and Chester, and more recently to 140 young people, politicians & civil servants at the Kew COP30 Youth Event in London. The Youth Board work on and promote the importance of EDI including, advocating for a ZSL internal EDI Network for young staff and volunteers and sitting on a working group focused on strengthening pathways into ZSL for those from underrepresented backgrounds. London Wildlife Trust Youth Board and Youth Work Our Youth Board is a group of 15 young people, aged 16-25, who advise the Trust on how to make their plans, projects and the internal running of the Trust accessible for young people. They are as representative as possible of young people in London. Their role is embedded in our governance structure and they advise on topics ranging from project work to sustainability policies. Field Studies Council Field Studies Council’s Youth Panel is an advisory panel of 12 young people aged 16-25, including members from underrepresented groups - women and non-binary individuals, and individuals who identify as disabled and global majority. Members are provided with a thorough induction to organisational processes and charity context to enable their meaningful and informed participation in decision-making and consultations. The Youth Panel will begin to contribute to the Field Studies Council’s organisational Working Groups including the Environment and Sustainability Working Group. Loch Lomond & The Trossachs National Park Authority (Youth Committee and programmes) Loch Lomond and The Trossachs National Park Youth Committee is a group of dedicated young people aged between 14-26 years old who are passionate about the National Park and making a difference. The committee was formed in 2018 to ensure that young people have a voice in the decision making of the National Park Authority, now and for the future. Youth Committee members help make decisions on key issues including climate change, how land is used, improving public transport for rural communities and green career opportunities. Members gain work experience, participate in National Park Board meetings, help shape National Park policies and develop youth-led projects. Pembrokeshire Coast National Park Authority (PCNPA) Next Generation PCNPA’s Youth Committee and Next Generation Youth Manifesto provide the youth voice in PCNPA member meetings and the PCNPA partnership forum influencing decision-making within the Park Authority and ensuring youth views are embedded. New Forest Youth Board The New Forest Youth Board is a group of 18 to 30 year olds with a shared passion for nature and connection to the New Forest. Our mission is to represent the voices of young people; to shape the future of the New Forest. The Board has contributed to the governance and management of the National Park through contributing to the New Forest Local Plan, the New Forest Partnership Plan, and the governance and delivery group for the Youth for Climate and Nature (YouCAN) scheme. BTO Youth BTO Youth engages a young audience with birds and nature; the Youth Advisory Panel gives youth input to the Trust on its work, and runs various programmes including an equipment donation scheme & Youth in Nature Summits; the Youth Representatives organise bird, nature and mental health related events in their local community. One of our core principles is for all our work to be youth-led, ensuring young people are always involved in decision-making and are always listened to. Our 2023-28 strategy outlines ways in which this is monitored and demonstrates that volunteers feel we are successful in ensuring this. Other contributions from: UK Man and the Biosphere Youth Network, Youngwilders CIC, Yorkshire Dales National Park Authority (youth focused programmes), CPRE, North York Moors National Park Authority (youth focused programmes), Target 23: Ensure Gender Equality and a Gender-Responsive Approach for Biodiversity ActionBTO Youth Guaranteed interviews for youth volunteers from under-represented groups and backgrounds – this includes recognising that women are often under-represented in this sector. Reserva: The Youth Land Trust Reserva has gender parity on our Board of Directors, Youth Council, and all of our research expeditions. Our staff is 100% women. We require any project we conduct to evaluate gender bias and make a plan for the inclusion of women and/or girls. Chester Zoo (Youth Board) The Chester Zoo Youth Board’s People and Culture working group contribute to discussions, reviews and strategies to promote and ensure gender-equality, as well as other under-represented communities, are included and valued in biodiversity action. Helmholtz Research Field Earth and Environment
Helmholtz Earth & Environment
Marie Heidenreich
marie.heidenreich@gfz.de
Commitment from the Helmholtz Research Field Earth and Environment towards the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework

Commitments_GBF_HelmholtzEarthAndEnvironment_2026.pdf Commitment from the Helmholtz Research Field Earth and Environment towards the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework
The Helmholtz Association of German Research Centers is Germany's largest research organization. With 18 centers, around 46,000 employees, and an annual budget of approximately 6.3 billion euros (2023), it is also one of the largest scientific research organizations worldwide.
In the Helmholtz Research Field Earth and Environment, scientists investigate the natural foundations of life on our planet—from the land surface to the oceans to the polar regions—in order to better understand the causes and consequences of global environmental changes such as climate change, species extinction, environmental pollution, and natural hazards, and to provide scientifically robust knowledge, innovative technologies, and concrete recommendations to policymakers, industry, and society.
The Helmholtz Forum Earth and Environment is the central platform for research synthesis and stakeholder dialogue in the Helmholtz Research Field Earth and Environment. The Forum bundles the expertise of the seven Helmholtz Earth and Environment Centers and acts as an interface between research, politics, and additional societal actors by specifically incorporating current findings from environmental and Earth system research into political discourse and highlighting evidence-based solutions and action options so that cutting-edge environmental research is effectively taken into account in public debates and decisions.
The following Centers are involved in the Helmholtz Research Field Earth and Environment: Alfred Wegener Institute, Helmholtz Centre for Polar and Marine Research (AWI)Forschungszentrum Jülich (FZJ)GEOMAR Helmholtz Centre for Ocean Research KielGFZ Helmholtz Centre for GeosciencesHelmholtz-Zentrum HereonHelmholtz Centre for Environmental Research – UFZKarlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT) The Helmholtz Association’s Research Field Earth and Environment submits this stakeholder commitment in the context of its joint research programme “Changing Earth – Sustaining our Future” (PoF IV, 2021–2027). Within this programme, we generate science-based understanding of biodiversity change and its links to ecosystem functioning by combining field studies and experiments with modelling, and by using taxonomic and functional metrics to characterize changes in biodiversity, detect shifts in community structure, relate these to ecosystem functioning, and link them to natural and anthropogenic drivers. A major contribution of the Helmholtz Research Field Earth and Environment towards the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework is the development of integrated approaches for managed land- and seascapes that quantify interactions between land-use change, biodiversity, and ecosystem functions/services. Results are incorporated into scenarios of selected elements of biodiversity that account for dispersal dynamics, trait diversity and species interactions, as well as policy options and management strategies for recent and novel ecosystems (including soil systems). In this context, the programme “Changing Earth – Sustaining our Future” develops novel land-use change pathways using model-based back-casting approaches in cooperation with social scientists. The Helmholtz Research Field strengthens observation capabilities through major infrastructures and coordinated monitoring. TERENO sites contribute to long-term observation of structures, functions and biodiversity across terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems and enable research on, among others, long-term effects of land-use change on biodiversity and related hydrological and biogeochemical processes. MOSES provides modular, highly flexible and mobile observation systems designed to unravel impacts of short-term events; it observes catchment-wide fluxes of energy, water and nutrients triggered by extreme hydrological events, including inland-to-coastal linkages, and supports event-oriented observation campaigns whose datasets are processed in close cooperation with programme topics. Mechanistic components such as MOBICOS riverine mesocosms enable the study of ecological responses along natural and anthropogenic impact gradients and help to investigate hydrological and biogeochemical cause–effect chains and feedbacks. The programme also expands activities towards the European-scale monitoring network eLTER, enabling effective monitoring of temporal variations and long-term trends in the context of biodiversity-relevant change across Europe. Topic 6 “Marine and Polar Life: Sustaining Biodiversity, Biotic Interactions and Biogeochemical Functions” provides the scientific foundation for sustainable ocean management by determining the functions and dynamics of marine ecosystems and assessing options to remedy and mitigate human impacts. It studies the diversity, structure and functioning of the marine biosphere, investigates how marine organisms and ecosystems adapt and respond to anthropogenic perturbations, and assesses to what extent carbon dioxide uptake by the marine biosphere and biogeochemical cycles are affected by climate change. Research covers all levels of biological integration from genes to ecosystems and integrates knowledge on the ocean system from the equator to the poles. Using compatible sampling and experimental approaches across polar, temperate and tropical habitats, the Helmholtz scientists resolve unifying principles of organismal and community responses to environmental change and their role in shaping biogeography and species interactions, based on taxonomic and trait-based groupings. It addresses key drivers such as warming, sea-ice decline, decreasing pH values and oxygen contents, resource extraction, fisheries, and pollution. Ecological, evolutionary and biogeochemical approaches are integrated to assess responses of marine life via adaptation, range shifts and community turnover, and to determine how these responses affect food webs, carbon turnover and other element cycles, including the biological carbon pump and ocean productivity. Strategies for marine governance and conservation are evaluated by assessing the biological and biogeochemical consequences of ocean change. The Helmholtz Institute for Functional Marine Biodiversity at the University of Oldenburg (HIFMB), strengthens integrated research on marine biodiversity and its importance for ecosystem functioning by integrating spatial patterns and temporal change, contributing to marine nature conservation and ecosystem management. The researchers of the Helmholtz Research Field further contribute biodiversity-relevant evidence by developing novel monitoring and assessment approaches for biota, chemicals and particles in environmental compartments (terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems including surface waters and deep sea, sediment, and interfaces) and by integrating non-chemical stressors (including climate-driven changes such as heat extremes) as integral parts of monitoring, including a monitoring toolbox for large-scale assessment of complex contamination and its natural attenuation and approaches to address multiple-stressor combinations at landscape scales.

IBCSD
Other
Indah Budiani
indah.budiani@ibcsd.or.id; info@ibcsd.or.id
Business Leadership for Biodiversity: Scaling Corporate Nature-Positive Action in Indonesia (2026–2030)

(a) Overview of Scope and Proposed Actions Indonesia Business Council for Sustainable Development (IBCSD) commits to support the implementation of the Convention on Biological Diversity and the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF) by enabling Indonesian companies to: • Identify and manage biodiversity-related risks and dependencies, • Integrate biodiversity into corporate decision-making, • Promote investment-ready nature-positive projects, • Strengthen access to biodiversity finance, • Align corporate action with Indonesia’s National Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan (NBSAP).
IBCSD will serve as: 1. A national business platform translating GBF targets into corporate tools, policy engagement, and capacity-building programs; and 2. As an emerging regional business leadership platform for biodiversity action in Southeast Asia, facilitating knowledge exchange and scalable private-sector models.
(b) Direct and Indirect Drivers Addressed This commitment addresses key direct drivers of biodiversity loss, including land-use change, ecosystem degradation, unsustainable resource extraction, and supply chain impacts linked to industrial and agricultural activities. It also tackles indirect drivers by strengthening corporate awareness of biodiversity-related risks, improving the integration of biodiversity considerations into financial and investment decision-making, and advocating for stronger enabling conditions to mobilize private sector finance for nature-positive action.

Asma Jeitani
A.Jeitani@cgiar.org
International collections of plant genetic resources maintained by the Centers of the CGIAR are conserved long term according to international standards and best practices

The genebanks operated by CGIAR Centers are custodians of the world’s most diverse publicly available crop germplasm collections. Located in diversity hotspots, they hold more than 700,000 accessions of more than 3,000 plant species. The majority of these accessions were collected prior to the Convention on Biological Diversity. The genetic material is freely available to researchers, breeders, local communities, and farmers under the terms of the International Treaty on Plant Genetic Resources for Food and Agriculture (Plant Treaty), helping them find their own solutions for a more resilient, sustainable agriculture. By the end of 2024, the percentage of accessions that were available from the collections was 87%. From 2022 to 2024, the genebanks distributed 337,330 germplasm samples to 123 countries. Forty-six percent went to users outside CGIAR, primarily to advanced research institutes, universities, and national breeding programs, the majority of them in developing countries. All distributions underwent rigorous controls in Germplasm Health Units (GHUs) and were compliant with national and international laws and policies.
