roshni.kutty@kalpavriksh.org
Preparation of People's Marine Biodiversity Register by Indigenous People and Local Communities

For the last 5 years Friends of Marine Life (FML) team has been documenting the in-depth knowledge of indigenous ‘Mukkuva’ fishing community [Element 6]. This includes their knowledge on marine biodiversity, seabed-related geographical and bathymetric knowledge, fishing and related astronomical knowledge [Element 3 and 4]. FML has documented 1683 sq. kms. of the Mukkuvar’s customary area, which includes the sea bed abutting the coasts of Kollam, Kerala to Kanyakumari, Tamil Nadu. This area abounds in rocky reefs and has rich marine biodiversity. So far, no serious seabed studies have been carried out. The impetus for this documentation came due to the exclusion of their knowledge on marine species from the marine biodiversity register prepared by the Kerala State Biodiversity Board in 2017, with the participation of the local community (KSBB. 2017. Marine Biodiversity Register Thiruvananthapuram. ISBN: 978-81-932596-4-1). The study began with identifying more than hundred indigenous and local knowledge holders and trained some of them in underwater diving and photography. Through this process, FML has documented nearly hundred seabed ecosystems and more than a thousand marine species with scientific details which is proposed to be published in early 2026. This documentation is carried out with the intent to help identify marine biodiversity hotspots that require urgent protection along the coast of Kollam, Kerala to Kanyakumari, Tamil Nadu. [Element 1 and 2].
GBF-INDICATOR-C.2
Keidanren Nature Conservation Council
KNCC
Japan
Arisa Okuda
kncf@keidanren.or.jp
The Action Plan for Nature Positive

(a) 1. Contributions through the Keidanren Nature Conservation Fund: GBF Target 19 (1) Support for NGOs projects that contribute to achieve the GBF targets Since the establishment of the Keidanren Nature Conservation Council in 1992, over the 32 years from fiscal 1993, we have annually called on Keidanren member companies (approximately 1,500 companies) for donations and collected contributions from member companies and their officers and employees. As a result, the Keidanren Nature Conservation Fund has supported a cumulative total of 1,782 projects, with total support amounting to approximately 5.3 billion yen. The areas of activity supported include natural resource management related to biodiversity conservation and protection of rare plants and animals, as well as afforestation and environmental education. From fiscal 2023, as a new grant policy of the fund, we have set a policy to support activities that contribute to the GBF and added a requirement in the application to clarify which of the 23 GBF targets the activity contributes to. (2) Fund for Community Development and Knowledge Management for the Satoyama Initiative (COMDEKS) COMDEKS (Community Development and Knowledge Management for the Satoyama Initiative) is a program established by the Japan Biodiversity Fund, which was set up in June 2011 with contributions from the Japanese government, to support on-the-ground activities in natural environments that have been maintained and managed by humans, such as rice paddies and reservoirs (so-called secondary natural environments). COMDEKS contributes to achieving the GBF by supporting developing countries’ 30by30 targets, promoting Nature-based Solutions (NbS), and advancing the SDGs, including using OECM (Other Effective Area-based Conservation Measures outside protected areas contributing to biodiversity conservation). The Keidanren Nature Conservation Fund announced at COP15 that it would jointly fund COMDEKS Phase 4 with the Ministry of the Environment. Since fiscal 2022, KNCC has been contributing 50 million yen annually, and the total contribution is planned to reach 300 million yen over five years. (3) Monitor the progress of the grant projects including on site monitoring Every year, we conduct site visits to observe support projects in Japan and overseas. The Chair of the Keidanren Nature Conservation Council and representatives of member companies participate, engaging in dialogue with local communities, governments, and municipalities to promote mutual understanding. The most recent site visit in Japan took place in August 2025 on the Enshunada Coast in Shizuoka Prefecture, where we visited organizations working to protect loggerhead turtles and coastal plants. The international site visit was held in July 2024 in Jakarta, West Java, where we visited a mangrove forest conservation project and deepened direct communication with local NGOs. 2. Promotion for nature positive management: GBF Targets 3, 8, 11, 15, 20, 21 (1) Revise Keidanren Declaration for Biodiversity and promote Keidanren Initiative for Biodiversity Conservation in response to the GBF In December 2023, we revised the Keidanren Declaration for Biodiversity and Guideline based on the GBF and NBSAP. The declaration and action guideline specify the vision, corporate roles, perspectives, and actions to promote the conservation of biodiversity by companies and the creation of a sustainable society, contributing to the GBF. Even after the revision of the declaration and action guideline, we continue to call for support. As of the end of December 2025, 378 companies and organizations have endorsed it, and 205 case studies of initiatives are posted on the website. Keidanren Declaration for Biodiversity and Guideline / Keidanren Nature Conservation Fund Keidanren Initiative for Biodiversity Conservation (2) Promote mainstreaming disclosure such as nature-related financial information The Keidanren Nature Conservation Committee participates in the TNFD Forum and plays the role of promoting understanding of the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) within Japan as co-convener of the TNFD Consultation Group of Japan. While taking GBF Target 15 into account, it has continuously promoted information disclosure activities aligned with the TNFD framework even before the TNFD Recommendation were officially published. As a result, as of the end of October 2025, the number of Japanese TNFD adopters reached 210 companies (of which about 30% are KNCC member companies), the highest in the world. (3) Call out for corporate efforts that contribute to the GBF and National Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan such as OECM and NbS We continuously provide member companies with information on national policies based on the GBF and NBSAP, encourage actions aligned with these policies, and offer opportunities to deepen insights through dialogue with academia. While being mindful of GBF Target 3 and Target 8, we promote initiatives related to OECM and NbS in companies, with KNCC serving as an initiator of the 30by30 Alliance established by the Ministry of the Environment to achieve the 30by30 target, and with the KNCC Chair as the chair of the Green Infrastructure Public-Private Partnership Platform, which is administered by the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism. (4) Provide information and raise awareness through symposiums, etc. Aiming to further promote nature-positive management, we collaborate with the Japan Conference for 2030 Global Biodiversity Framework (J-GBF), for which the Ministry of the Environment serves as the secretariat, to co-host annual symposiums and networking events between companies and NGOs, contributing to GBF Target 21. 3. Facilitation of the environment for nature positive management in Japan and overseas: GBF Targets 3, 8, 11, 14, 15 (1) Make policy recommendations to facilitate nature positive management With a view to the interim assessment of the GBF to be conducted at COP17, in November 2025, we published the Proposal for Integrating Biodiversity and Natural Capital Conservation with Sustainable Economic Growth. When the KNCC Chair handed the proposal to the Minister of the Environment and others, discussions were held on the ideal approaches and necessary measures for companies to contribute to the GBF and grow in a sustainable manner. For details of the Proposal for Integrating Biodiversity and Natural Capital Conservation with Sustainable Economic Growth, please refer to the links below. Outline - Integrating Biodiversity and Natural Capital Conservation with Sustainable Economic GrowthMain text – Integrating Biodiversity and Natural Capital Conservation with Sustainable Economic GrowthExamples of Integrated Actions for Biodiversity and Natural Capital ConservationIn addition, our organization serves as a committee member for committees leaded by the Ministry of the Environment and the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism, where we provide input on building the necessary foundations for realizing a nature-positive economy and on measures to support companies. Specific examples include: Committee on NBSAP DevelopmentStudy Group on Nature Positive EconomyCommittee to draft the Basic Policy on the Promotion of Activities to Enhance Regional BiodiversityCommittee to draft the Basic Policy for the Preservation of Green Spaces and the Promotion of Greening in Cities(2) Participate in international projects and initiatives on biodiversity, and formulate frameworks of biodiversity; (i)Information disclosure (TNFD) (ii)Data infrastructure development (iii)OECM To steadily promote efforts toward GBF Target 15 while strengthening international collaboration, we encourage Japanese companies to actively participate in international discussions on topics such as creating guidance for nature-positive management led by overseas business organizations, or pilot programs on transition plans and nature-related data infrastructure led by TNFD. In addition, to achieve GBF Target 3, we have made efforts to spread private-sector initiatives for OECM, through collaborative activities with overseas expert organizations. 4. Collecting the latest trends overseas / Dissemination of efforts by Japanese businesses: GBF Target 21 (1) Dispatch missions to survey global trends CBD COP16: KNCC has participated in all meetings since COP9IUCN World Conservation Congress (October 2025): KNCC has participated in every event since the first congressIUCN Leaders Forum (October 2023, October 2024): KNCC has participated in every event since the first forum(2) Disseminate case studies and opinions of the Japanese business community and companies through presentations at various major conferences, and through dialogues with major organizations COP16: Co-hosted side events related to G7ANPE, IAS, and COMDEKS with the Ministry of the Environment and major international organizations. Multiple Japanese companies gave speeches at the Business and Biodiversity Forum, Finance and Biodiversity Day, and Science-Policy Forum for Biodiversity.International Union for the Conservation of Nature (IUCN) WCC: In the opening session of the Business Forum and other IUCN-hosted events, many Japanese companies gave speeches, showcasing the Japanese business community’s efforts in connection with topics such as resource mobilization for biodiversity and OECM. KNCC also hosted an event, presenting private sector-led OECM initiatives in the Asia region.HLPF Side Event: COMDEKS-related events have been held every year since 2023 in collaboration with the Ministry of the Environment, UNDP, and others.Dialogue sessions with major organizations during international conferences or opportunities when they visit Japan: BirdLife International, CBD, IUCN, TNFD, UNDP, WBCSD, WEF, WWF, etc.5. PDCA: GBF Target 21 (1) Monitoring the progress of the action plan and implementation of improvement measures based on progress results Since 2011, we have conducted the Questionnaire survey on corporate biodiversity actions in Japan almost every year to grasp the progress of efforts by Japan’s business community. From the 2022 survey (with results published in 2023), the questions were revised to also reflect the GBF goals, and the survey has continued to be conducted. For the latest survey results, please refer to the link below. https://www.keidanren.net/kncf/en/committee/questionnaire (2) Intermediate evaluation for a global review at CBD COP17 We submitted this NSA report and provided the government with feedback on the intermediate evaluation of the NBSAP and the National Report. (b) Our fund supports the activities of NGOs that are well acquainted with local natural environments and ecosystem characteristics to improve direct drivers such as changes in land and sea use, climate change, and invasive species. Example 1. Community-led Reforestation to Restore Tropical Peat-Swamp Forest in Central Kalimantan, Indonesia Degraded peatlands lose their resistance to fires, which increases the frequency of forest fires and leads to damage to human health and the emission of greenhouse gases. In this project, the organization aims to restore forests by leading and supporting conservation activities by residents through the establishment and promotion of tree nursery cultivation as a sustainable livelihood. [Borneo Nature Foundation, Indonesia; 2nd year] Example 2. Nature Restoration of Satoyama Waterside by Controlling Crayfish Aiming to establish efficient and effective methods of controlling American crayfish, an invasive species that reduces aquatic flora and fauna, capture tests are being conducted under various environmental conditions using new continuous trapping devices and artificial aquatic plants. In addition, we are engaged in monitoring and verifying the recovery of ecosystems following a decline in crayfish population density, and in raising awareness and disseminating control technologies through symposiums and other means. [Society for Shinaimotsugo Conservation, Japan; 3rd year] For details on other support projects, please refer to the link below. https://www.keidanren.net/kncf/en/fund/projects

Konservasi Indonesia
KI
Diah Fitri Ekarini
dekarini@konservasi-id.org

Advancing integrated landscape and seascape conservation through science-based partnerships, ecosystem restoration, climate resilience, and sustainable management to deliver long-term benefits for people and nature in Indonesia

Konservasi Indonesia (KI) commits to advancing integrated landscape and seascape conservation across Indonesia through science-based, partnership-driven approaches that protect biodiversity, strengthen climate resilience, and ensure sustainable resource management. In alignment with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework/KMGBF, KI works collaboratively with national and subnational governments, Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities, civil society, and the private sector to deliver nature-based solutions that generate long-term benefits for both people and nature. To achieve this, KI implements its Strategy to 2030 through the following priority pillars: Protection and Restoration of High-Value Ecosystems Protecting and restoring mangrove forests, peatlands, and tropical rainforests that support biodiversity, ecosystem services, and community livelihoods. This includes scaling up ecosystem restoration, strengthening the management effectiveness of protected and conserved areas, and promoting sustainable land-use practices at landscape level.Climate Change Resilience and Adaptation Enhancing the resilience of ecosystems and communities to climate-related risks through nature-based solutions, ecosystem-based adaptation approaches, and support to government-led climate mitigation and adaptation initiatives.Marine Protection and Fisheries Improvement Expanding and strengthening the effective management of Marine Protected Areas (MPAs), improving fisheries governance and sustainability, and enhancing the socio-economic well-being of coastal and fishing communities through sustainable marine resource management.All actions are underpinned by robust scientific data, inclusive governance, cross-sectoral partnerships, capacity building, and innovative financing mechanisms to ensure measurable, scalable, and lasting impacts.
KI’s commitment addresses both direct and indirect drivers of biodiversity loss, as identified in global assessments including those of the Intergovernmental Science-Policy Platform on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services (IPBES). KI will address the following direct pressures on biodiversity: Land- and sea-use change Including deforestation, peatland degradation, mangrove conversion, and unsustainable coastal development, through integrated landscape and seascape planning, ecosystem restoration, and improved protected and conserved area management.Overexploitation of natural resources Particularly unsustainable fisheries and extractive practices, by strengthening fisheries governance, promoting sustainable harvest practices, and improving compliance and monitoring systems.Climate change impacts By enhancing ecosystem-based adaptation, restoring carbon-rich ecosystems (e.g., peatlands and mangroves), and strengthening climate resilience for vulnerable communities and ecosystems.Ecosystem degradation Through restoration efforts and improved management effectiveness in terrestrial and marine conservation areas.KI’s commitment also addresses underlying systemic drivers and enabling conditions by: Strengthening governance and cross-sectoral coordination Supporting integrated, multi-level, and cross-jurisdictional planning and policy coherence.Enhancing community participation and inclusive decision-making Empowering Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities in biodiversity management and sustainable livelihood initiatives.Improving scientific knowledge and data availability Promoting evidence-based decision-making through biodiversity monitoring, spatial planning tools, and applied research.Mobilizing innovative and sustainable financing Leveraging blended finance, public-private partnerships, and performance-based mechanisms to scale conservation impact.Building institutional and local capacity Supporting long-term institutional strengthening for adaptive and effective ecosystem management.

Maailman Luonnon Säätiö - World Wide Fund For Nature, Suomen rahasto sr
WWF Finland
Sini Halm
info@wwf.fi; sini.halm@wwf.fi
Oil Spill Preparedness

WWF Finland is part of the global WWF network, working to halt biodiversity loss and safeguard ecosystems on land and at sea. In Finland, we combine science-based conservation, preparedness, policy influence and citizen engagement to protect nature from acute and long-term threats. Marine and coastal biodiversity is particularly vulnerable to oil spills, which can cause severe and long-lasting damage to species, habitats and ecosystem functioning. Preventing and mitigating oil spill impacts is therefore essential for protecting biodiversity, reducing environmental risks and strengthening ecosystem resilience, especially in sensitive marine areas such as the Baltic Sea. WWF Finland trains and maintains a nationwide network of volunteer oil spill responders. Through structured training programmes, exercises and refresher courses, volunteers are equipped with the skills needed for shoreline clean-up and the rescue and care of oil-affected wildlife. The training builds operational readiness and ensures rapid, organised support in the event of an oil spill. This commitment directly reduces the risks and impacts of oil spills on biodiversity by strengthening preparedness and response capacity. Rapid, well-coordinated volunteer action helps limit damage to coastal ecosystems, protect vulnerable species and support ecosystem recovery following pollution events. By investing in training and readiness, WWF Finland contributes to the protection of marine biodiversity and to broader environmental risk management.
GBF-INDICATOR-7.1; GBF-INDICATOR-7.2
Invasive Species Management

WWF Finland is part of the global WWF network, working to halt biodiversity loss by protecting ecosystems, species and ecological processes. In Finland, we combine science-based conservation with policy influence and citizen engagement to address both long-term and acute pressures on nature. Invasive alien species are a major driver of biodiversity loss, degrading habitats, displacing native species and weakening ecosystem resilience. Their spread is often accelerated by human activity, making early detection, rapid response and broad public participation essential for effective biodiversity protection. WWF Finland coordinates invasive species management through a combination of volunteer action and digital engagement. We organise and support volunteer activities focused on the removal and control of invasive species in vulnerable habitats. In addition, we use a dedicated game-based mobile application to engage the public, encourage participation, and lower the threshold for contributing to invasive species detection and removal efforts. This commitment directly addresses a key pressure on biodiversity by reducing the spread and impacts of invasive species. Volunteer action and digital participation help protect native species and habitats, prevent further ecological degradation, and strengthen ecosystem resilience. By combining hands-on management with innovative public engagement tools, WWF Finland increases both the scale and effectiveness of invasive species control and supports long-term biodiversity protection.
KMGBF-INDICATOR-BIN-6; GBF-INDICATOR-6.1
International Biodiversity Funding

WWF Finland is part of the global WWF network, working to halt biodiversity loss by protecting ecosystems, species and ecological processes. As part of this work, WWF Finland supports biodiversity conservation internationally by mobilising and channelling public funding to programmes implemented with partners in developing countries. WWF Finland receives development cooperation funding from the Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland which is channelled through WWF Finland to programme partners for biodiversity conservation and sustainable ecosystem management. To compliment the funding from the Finnish development cooperation funding (9,5 EUR million) WWF Finland provides self financing of approximately EUR 1 million which will be directed to biodiversity-related activities through these programmes. The funding supports actions aimed at protecting and restoring ecosystems, reducing pressures on biodiversity, and strengthening local capacity and ownership for sustainable natural resource management in 10 partner countries. Programme promotes inclusive conservation model, which is promoting human rights based approach, focuses on most vulnerable groups including indigenous people and local communities and promotes gender balance. Special attention has been focused on disability inclusion. Through this commitment, WWF Finland contributes to the effective allocation and use of international public biodiversity finance in support of national and international biodiversity objectives.
Bolivia (Plurinational State of); Indonesia; Lao People's Democratic Republic; Madagascar; Nepal; Uganda; United Republic of Tanzania; Ukraine
GBF-INDICATOR-D.3
Biodiversity Learning and Action

WWF Finland is part of the global WWF network, working to halt biodiversity loss and build a future in which people and nature live in balance. In Finland, we combine science-based conservation, policy influence and public engagement to drive lasting environmental change. Biodiversity underpins human wellbeing, climate resilience and economic stability. Its rapid loss is driven in part by everyday choices related to consumption, land use and energy. Empowering people with knowledge and practical tools is therefore essential to reducing pressures on nature and supporting long-term protection and restoration. WWF Finland will reach 58,000 individuals through WWF Ambassador–facilitated workshops focused on learning and action related to reducing ecological footprints, protecting and restoring nature, and understanding the links between biodiversity and climate change. The workshops translate scientific knowledge into practical, actionable steps that support lasting behaviour change and active environmental citizenship. This commitment addresses a key indirect driver of biodiversity loss: unsustainable human behaviour. By increasing biodiversity literacy and enabling people to act, the workshops help reduce pressures on ecosystems, support restoration and protection efforts, and strengthen societal capacity to respond to biodiversity- and climate-related risks.

From Awareness to Action: Empowering Hungarian Society for Biodiversity

NSC FOE HU commits to significantly enhancing the Hungarian public’s engagement and active participation in biodiversity conservation through nationwide awareness-raising and educational programs. The initiative focuses on disseminating practical methods for biodiversity-friendly gardening, sustainable municipal management, and nature-positive agricultural practices between July 1, 2025, and June 30, 2028.
Naturalis Biodiversity Center
William Voorberg
william.voorberg@naturalis.nl
Contributions of Naturalis Biodiversity Center to the KMGB Framework

Naturalis Biodiversity Center serves as the national biodiversity research institute of the Netherlands. Its mission is to advance knowledge on biodiversity and to strengthen societal awareness and appreciation of the natural world. Through its activities, Naturalis aims to generate scientific insight into nature, support evidence-informed decision-making, and foster public engagement with nature. In this context, Naturalis contributes to the implementation of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KMGBF) primarily in its role as a knowledge partner. While these contributions are largely indirect, they are diverse and substantive in scope. Under this commitment, we report on the contributions of 14 distinct projects to KMGBF targets. Given the diversity of these initiatives, each project is presented separately. Naturgy Energy Group
Naturgy
Spain
Nieves Cifuentes Valero
ncifuentes@naturgy.com; bestebanv@naturgy.com
Corporate Integration of Biodiversity and Nature-Related Risk Management within Naturgy’s 2025–2027 Strategic Cycle

For the period 2025–2027, Naturgy commits to integrating biodiversity and the management of nature-related risks and dependencies into its corporate governance, sustainability strategy and energy transition roadmap, contributing to the implementation of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework. Through the application of the LEAP approach (Locate, Evaluate, Assess and Prepare), Naturgy has identified climate change, water use and land occupation associated with the development and operation of energy infrastructure—particularly renewable generation and electricity networks—as its main drivers of impact and dependency on nature. On this basis, Naturgy integrates biodiversity into strategic planning and decision-making processes and implements its Sustainability Plan 2025–2027 through actions structured at three levels: Integration into governance and corporate management Oversight by governing bodies.Improvement of systems for identifying and assessing nature-related risks and impacts, incorporated into the Group’s global risk map.Certified environmental management systems (ISO 14001) covering the vast majority of operational assets.Annual reporting of progress within the framework of European sustainability regulation.Reduction of key pressures on biodiversity Progress towards decarbonization targets, reducing climate-related pressures on ecosystems.Monitoring and progressive improvement in water management and efficiency, particularly in generation activities.Consideration of environmental criteria in the design and development of new projects to minimize direct and indirect impacts on biodiversity.Direct action on biodiversity and ecosystems Systematic application of the mitigation hierarchy (avoid, minimize, restore and offset impacts).Development and implementation of specific biodiversity enhancement initiatives across the Group’s assets.Annual monitoring of the number and scope of such initiatives.This commitment reflects the progressive integration of biodiversity into Naturgy’s business model and contributes to the effective incorporation of nature considerations within the energy sector.

Netherlands Food Partnership
NFP
Mariëlle Karssenberg
mkarssenberg@nlfoodpartnership.com; info@nlfoodpartnership.com
Netherlands Food Partnership - Biodiversity & Food Systems Partnership

The Biodiversity & Food Systems Partnership is a global, multi-stakeholder community of practice, convened and facilitated by the Netherlands Food Partnership, that connects biodiversity and food systems actors across public sector, private sector, civil society organisations, research institutions and financial sectors as well as different scales. It operates globally, with specific focus on low and middle incomecontext and countries in the Global South, and contributes to implementation of the Global Biodiversity Framework by advancing integrated biodiversity–food systems thinking and approaches. The partnership addresses the systemic interlinkages between biodiversity loss and food systems transformation. It aims to catalyze a shift from siloed biodiversity and food security agendas toward integrated, mutually reinforcing strategies. The partnership works along three reinforcing dimensions: Mental models Reframing biodiversity as a foundational leverage point for resilient, nutritious and equitable food systems. Positioning food systems as a key entry point for biodiversity regeneration and biodiversity as a key entrypoint for sustainable food systems.Relationships and coalitions Building trust-based, cross-sector and cross-scale collaborations that anticipate and manage synergies and trade-offs between biodiversity conservation and food and nutrition security.Practices, policies and investment Supporting a safe dialogue space for interactive learning to support development of integrated solutions, policy coherence and aligned resource flows that combine biodiversity and food systems objectives.Core actions Multi-stakeholder learning and agenda setting Facilitation of a global learning space with over 150 organizations.Co-creation of shared agendas, narratives and strategic priorities.Quarterly network dialogues and targeted working groups collaborations on identified points of collective actions.Use case development and knowledge tools Development and dissemination of biodiversity–food systems use cases across public, private and civil society actors. Sharing of practical lessons on key bottlenecks in managing biodiversity and food systems integration in practice.Creation of facilitation tools and guidance for strengthening the integration of biodiversity into food systems policies, value chains, finance and development programs through facilitated dialogue.Coordinated messaging and policy engagement Development of shared messages to inform national, regional and international processes as well as other networks of interest for biodiversity and food systems integration.Strategic collaborative engagement in relevant global moments, including CBD COPs and related science-policy processes.Initiatives to integrate boidiversity-food systems linkages. Structured support to food security programs to integrate biodiversity–food systems linkages.In-country and/or sub-national co-creation workshops to identify context-specific opportunities for biodiversity-food systems integration that are aligned with national biodiversity and food systems strategies.Catalyzing new or strengthened initiatives that explicitly contribute to biodiversity targets while advancing food systems strategies in LMIC countries.Below a description of the direct and indirect drivers or enabling conditions of biodiversity loss the commitment will seek to address. Sectoral silos and fragmented governance The partnership directly tackles fragmentation between biodiversity and food systems communities by creating structured, facilitated collaboration spaces across sectors and scales. Norms, narratives and mental models By shifting perceptions of biodiversity from a constraint to a foundation for food system resilience and sustainability, the partnership addresses and challenges underlying mental models that prevent transformation. Capacity and knowledge gaps Through tools, incubator processes and shared learning, it strengthens technical, strategic and collaborative capacity across actors across sectors and scales. The Netherlands Food Partnership Biodiversity & Food Systems Partnership therefore contributes to the GBF mission by: Catalyzing integrated action across sectors in the food systems that are central to biodiversity loss.Supporting mainstreaming of biodiversity across agriculture, finance, nutrition and development agendas.Strengthening enabling conditions, including collaboration, shared narratives, innovation, and alignment of investment and policy for biodiversity and food systems integration.In doing so, it helps operationalize transformative change in line with the GBF vision of living in harmony with nature by 2050

Nordea Bank Apb
Financial institution
Anna-Karin Modin Edman
anna-karin.modin-edman@nordea.com
Nordea´s thematic guideline on biodiversity

Nordea’s position on biodiversity is described in our Thematic Guideline on Biodiversity where we outline our commitments to high-level actions to incorporate biodiversity into our business, including risk management, strategy and offerings as part of a roadmap 2023-2025. The purpose of Nordea’s biodiversity guideline is to set out Nordea’s position on biodiversity and describe its current work and commitments to further action to improve our performance on this topic. It forms the basis for dialogue with our stakeholders on this aspect of our business. Nordea’s business includes financial services, such as corporate and household lending, wealth management, investments, and advisory services for corporate, private and institutional clients, as well as own operations. The guideline applies to all markets in which Nordea operates or conducts business and includes its value chain. However, we acknowledge the challenges inherent in adopting a value chain perspective, given the current limited availability of biodiversity methodologies, tools and data. As value chain data availability and quality improve, we will refine our approach. When assessing and addressing biodiversity, we include terrestrial, freshwater and marine ecosystems. We acknowledge the importance of the five main drivers of biodiversity loss globally as defined by the IPBES: land and sea use change, over-exploitation, pollution, climate change and invasive alien species. We will update our position in 2026 to reflect the nature-related priorities in Nordea’s 2030 strategy and evolving nature-related risk agenda.
Ocean & Climate Platform (Plateforme Océan & Climat)
OCP (POC)
Marine Lecerf
mlecerf@ocean-climate.org
Ocean Breakthroughs

Launched in 2023, the Ocean Breakthroughs were developed by experts from the ocean community – united under the Marrakech Partnership on Ocean & Coastal Zones and supported by the UN High-Level Climate Champions – to accelerate action and investment in ocean-based climate action. Coordinated by the Ocean & Climate Platform, they now act as the umbrella initiative of the Global Climate Action Agenda. The Ocean Breakthroughs establish 2030 science-based targets across five sectors – marine conservation, ocean renewable energy, shipping, aquatic foods, and coastal tourism – designed to unlock transformative change at the scale of entire sectors. Importantly, these targets provide a pathway to scale ocean-based solutions in a way that advances both nature and climate targets, ensuring actions are holistic, effective and equitable. While the targets were initially developed to catalyse actions from Non-State actors, they act as a compass for all. Governments can use the Breakthroughs as an effective tool to develop and implement ocean-based measures in their strategies. Details for each sector are as follows: 1) Marine Conservation Breakthrough: By 2030, investments of at least USD 72 billion secure the integrity of ocean ecosystems by protecting, restoring, and conserving at least 30% of the ocean for the benefit of people, climate, and nature. In parallel, additional ecosystem-focused breakthroughs have emerged in recent years including those targeting mangroves, coral reefs, seagrass, saltmarsh, seaweed, and kelp. While not formally included in this commitment, these are highly relevant to the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) and the goals of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF). 2) Ocean Renewable Breakthrough: By 2030, install at least 380 GW of offshore capacity while establishing targets and enabling measures for net-positive biodiversity outcomes and advocate for mobilizing USD 10 billion in concessional finance for developing economies to reach that goal. 3) Shipping Breakthrough: by 2030, use zero emission fuels for at least 5%—aiming for 10%—of international shipping fuels and 15% of domestic shipping fuels; upskill and retrain 450,000 seafarers; ensure 30% trade moves through climate adapting ports; reduce the impact of international shipping on marine biodiversity by 30%. 4) Aquatic Food Breakthrough: By 2030, provide at least USD 4 billion per year to support resilient aquatic food systems that will contribute to healthy, regenerative ecosystems, and sustain food and nutrition security for three billion people. 5) Coastal Tourism Breakthrough: By 2030, USD 30 billion per year are invested to support halving emissions of coastal tourism; and additional investments are made to build the resilience of local communities, as well as to recover and protect ecosystems to sustainably manage tourism in island and coastal destinations most vulnerable to climate change. A concrete roadmap – known as the ‘Blue Package’ – was developed to translate the ambition of the Ocean breakthroughs into concrete action, providing actionable steps for countries, civil society, businesses, and communities to implement by 2028. Complementing this roadmap, a comprehensive set of resources has been created, ranging from policy briefs to technical guidelines. For instance, to address the need to mobilise and scale up investment in ocean-based climate solutions, an Ocean Investment Protocol has been developed. It provides a practical framework to align public and private finance toward a sustainable blue economy. Additionally, it includes annexes highlighting priority actions and case studies across the Ocean Breakthroughs sectors and demonstrating concrete solutions that are already generating impact. To ensure that ambition is matched by accountability, the Ocean Breakthroughs are supported by a dashboard – developed to track progress toward the 2030 targets. This online tool offers an overview of where progress is made, where gaps remain, where gaps remain and where action can make the greatest impact to inform decision-making. In the past few years, the Ocean Breakthroughs have been mainstreamed across the ocean agenda, including at the UNFCCC, CBD COP16, the 3rd UN Ocean Conference, and the 2025 IUCN World Conservation Congress — reflecting its ambition to serve the integrated achievement of global climate, biodiversity and sustainable development goals. Visit the website: ocean-breakthroughs.org

OroVerde - The Tropical Forst Foundation
Christian Cray
ccray@oroverde.de
Conservation of Amazon territories by strengthening indigenous communities

The aim of the project is to strengthen the territorial governance of indigenous peoples in Ecuador and Peru by highlighting their active contribution to biodiversity conservation and climate change mitigation, as well as through effective political influence, exchange of experiences, and national and international partnerships. To this end, the partner organizations are implementing elements of an indigenous economy and nature conservation rules from participatory community development plans, mainly with young adults and women, which then serve as successful and concrete examples for political work at the meso and macro levels. The examples and experiences of indigenous peoples' contribution to biodiversity conservation and climate change mitigation are systematized, processed, and disseminated and discussed in the project regions, nationally in both countries, and internationally. Access to an international network supports the monitoring of environmental conflicts and effective influence on political processes and instruments in Ecuador and Peru, so that these take greater account of the concerns and contributions of indigenous peoples to biodiversity conservation and climate change mitigation.

Biodiversity-friendly practices in the coffee, banana and cocoa sectors in Mexico, Guatemala and the Dominican Republic - "BioFinCas"

BioFinCas shows that our diet is directly linked to the health of our planet: when cocoa, coffee, and bananas are produced cheaply “in bulk,” monocultures and agrochemicals often promote deforestation—and thus biodiversity loss. At the same time, small and medium-sized producers bear a large share of the climate risks and costs (e.g., crop failures). BioFinCas wants to turn this around: Biodiversity-friendly, climate-resilient agriculture should become the new standard – with fair risk and profit distribution along the entire value chain. To this end, the project is working with umbrella organizations, financial institutions, insurance companies, politicians, and other stakeholders in Mexico, Guatemala, and the Dominican Republic to develop scalable risk finance solutions, strengthen capacities, and bring recommendations into the policy dialogue—supplemented by awareness and consumer campaigns at the EU level. https://www.iki-cac.org/en/IKI-in-the-region/large-scale-transformation-biodiversity-friendly-and-climate-resilient

Ensure that areas under agriculture, aquaculture, fisheries and forestry are managed sustainably, in particular through the sustainable use of biodiversity, including through a substantial increase of the application of biodiversity friendly practices

1. Sustainable Agricultural Areas Definition: Agricultural areas managed in ways that meet current food and economic needs without compromising the ability of future generations to do the same. This involves protecting soil, water, and biodiversity while reducing pollution and greenhouse gas emissions. Key features: • Efficient use of natural resources (e.g., water, soil nutrients) • Maintenance or improvement of soil health • Reduction in harmful inputs like chemical fertilizers or pesticides • Protection of surrounding ecosystems • Resilience to climate change Example: Crop rotation, agroforestry, organic farming, or integrated pest management on farmland. 2. Sustainable Forest Management Definition: A method of managing forests to preserve their ecological, economic, and social functions over the long term. It ensures forests remain healthy, productive, and capable of providing ecosystem services like carbon storage, clean water, and habitat for biodiversity. Key features: • Controlled and planned harvesting • Conservation of biodiversity • Reforestation or natural regeneration after logging • Protection of watersheds and soil • Respect for local communities and indigenous rights Example: Selective logging combined with replanting, rather than clear-cutting, and protecting old-growth areas.

Reduce pollution risks and the negative impact of pollution from all sources by 2030, to levels that are not harmful to biodiversity and ecosystem functions and services, considering cumulative effects, and in line with region’s jurisdiction.

Minimize the impact of climate change on biodiversity and increase its resilience through mitigation, adaptation, and disaster risk reduction actions, including through nature-based solution and/or ecosystem-based approaches
Identify and assess priority invasive alien species, control or eradicate them, and prevent their introduction

Contribute to ensuring that the harvesting and use of wild species is sustainable, legal, and safe for human health, by taking actions to support the implementation and enforcement of national policies and laws.

Restore: Return an ecosystem to its original, natural state before it was degraded or disturbed. Rehabilitate: Improve the functionality and health of an ecosystem, even if it doesn’t return to its original condition.
Decrease conversion of natural areas and maintain natural areas

Converted or transformed land refers to land that has been changed from its original or natural state to a different use, usually for human activities such as agriculture, urban development, forestry or industrial purposes. Common Types of Land Conversion or Transformation: 1. Forest to Farmland - Trees are cleared for crops or livestock grazing. - Common in tropical regions (e.g., Amazon deforestation for soy or cattle). 2. Farmland to Urban Areas - Agricultural land is developed into housing, commercial centers, or infrastructure. - Typical in expanding cities and suburbs. 3. Wetlands to Dry Land - Wetlands are drained for agriculture, construction, or roadways. - This can lead to habitat loss and flood risk. 4. Natural Grasslands to Plantations or Mines - Conversion for resource extraction (e.g., mining) or monoculture (e.g., palm oil plantations).
Increase protected areas and the extent of areas in the region that are conserved through Other Effective area-based Conservation Measures (OECMs)

CBD Decision 14/8, adopted at COP14, formally recognizes "other effective area-based conservation measures" (OECMs) alongside protected areas as key tools for biodiversity conservation. It provides guidance on identifying, governing, and reporting OECMs, emphasizing their role in delivering long-term in situ conservation, often led by indigenous peoples, local communities, or other non-state actors. The decision supports the integration of these areas into broader conservation strategies, contributing to global targets like Aichi Target 11 and laying groundwork for more inclusive and effective biodiversity protection.
SOS Mata Atlântica Foundation
SOSMA
Brazil
Diego Martinez
diego@sosma.org.br
Conservation, restoration and social engagement in two priority watersheds in the Brazilian Atlantic Forest

The Atlantic Forest covers approximately 15% of Brazil’s territory and is recognized as a global biodiversity hotspot due to its exceptionally high levels of species richness and endemism. However, this biome is also one of the most threatened in the world. Today, only about 24% of its original forest cover remains, and 95% of the existing forest fragments are smaller than 5 hectares, resulting in severe habitat fragmentation and biodiversity loss. The Atlantic Forest is home to the majority of Brazil’s population and economic activity, concentrating around 80% of the national GDP and a large share of the country’s food production. As a result, there is an intense demand for ecosystem services such as water provision, climate regulation, soil protection, and pollination. The SOS Mata Atlântica Foundation was founded in 1986 with the mission of inspiring society to protect the Atlantic Forest. The Foundation's results include the restoration of more than 23,000 hectares of native forests, support for the management of over 2 million hectares of Protected Areas, and a strong advocacy that contributed to the approval of a Federal Law for protection and sustainable use of the Atlantic Forest. Currently, the Foundation’s strategic actions are focused on two priority watersheds: the Tietê and Paraíba do Sul river basins. This territory encompasses approximately 5.5 million hectares across 170 municipalities and reflects many of the key challenges faced by the Atlantic Forest today. These include historically degraded landscapes and deforestation rates that, althought reduced, still requires enforcement, coexisting with important forest remnants and regenerating ecosystems, as well as high pressure from urban and agricultural activities, and significant opportunities for mobilizing partnerships and investments in nature. Within this territory, SOS Mata Atlântica integrates efforts from its core programs—forest restoration, protected areas, water conservation, environmental education, partnership building, and public policy advocacy. These actions are guided by spatial analyses that identify priority areas and cost-effective solutions to enhance ecological connectivity, conserve biodiversity, and strengthen ecosystem services. With targets to 2030, this commitment aligns with several targets from the GBF and from Brazil's NBSAP, primarily seeking to address habitat loss and fragmentation, forest degradation, water pollution, and pressure on natural ecosystems resulting from urban expansion and intensive agricultural practices as direct drivers of biodiversity loss in the Atlantic Forest. The strategy also tackles important indirect drivers, such as weak land-use planning, insufficient enforcement of environmental legislation, limited ecological connectivity at the landscape scale, lack of awareness and engagement from society, and underinvestment in nature. Through this integrated territorial strategy, the Foundation aims to combine the results of on-the-ground actions (forest restoration, support for public protected areas and the creation of private reserves, promote social engagement, and leverage investments) with broader advocacy, knowledge and communication actions to create the enabling conditions for long-term biodiversity conservation and sustainable development in the Atlantic Forest.

Stiftung Artenschutz ( Species Conservation Foundation)
Carolin Klangwald
office@stiftung-artenschutz.de; carolin.klangwald@stiftung-artenschutz.de
EcoSounds Madagascar

Madagascar’s biodiversity is highly threatened. This is especially true for the Western dry deciduous forests of the island which suffer from illegal exploitation. Conservation measures which are in place might be successful but almost always lack continuous monitoring of success, because monitoring biodiversity and its change is a labour and often resource intensive task. The method of Passive Acoustic Monitoring (PAM) is gaining worldwide attention by research and conservation organizations as it can circumvent some of these difficulties. PAM uses autonomous recording units (ARU) to record the soundscape of a specific environment, which can be later analyzed for the presence of a single or multiple species, e.g., allowing the monitoring of entire bird communities. Although PAM is likely to change the future of conservation and biodiversity monitoring, it has been rarely used for the highly endemic fauna of the Madagascan peninsula due to remaining methodological difficulties. Such difficulties include, the creation of reference call libraries for endangered Malagasy taxa, the development of automatic algorithms to facilitate acoustic analysis, and the building of local capacity for applying the method in environmental monitoring programs. Our project supports the development of PAM for conservation in Madagascar using the Menabe Antimena Protected Area as starting point for creating a call reference library for Malagasy species and develop automatic approaches for species monitoring. The project can be extended to other areas in Madagascar and more endangered species. Furthermore, the project will build capacity in young researchers and local assistants in using PAM as part of the community based patrolling at Kirindy Forest.

GBF-INDICATOR-4.CT.4; GBF-INDICATOR-A.CT.5
Strengthening locally-led conservation to protect Sulawesi’s threatened yet overlooked wildlife

Sulawesi is home to myriads of endemic species, yet many are threatened due to habitat loss and hunting and have not received substantial conservation attention both in terms of research and conservation. PROGRES champions locally led conservation to offer long-lasting solutions to address this challenge. The approach is first, people-centered, meaning emphasize on the capacity building and institution development for the local community to run and lead conservation projects each in their own area and tailor our programs to match both wildlife need and community aspirations; second science-based, meaning we conduct ecological and socioeconomic research to understand the current state of wildlife and measure the impacts of our conservation programs. Different methods are used to study wildlife, and do Focus Group Discussion, community meetings, and community engagement and village-level activities as parts of our whole programs to build trust and relationship with the local community. The project also partners with other Indonesian and international experts, as well as Nature Conservation Agency to foster the conservation of biodiversity in Sulawesi. The partnership between PROGRES and Stiftung Artenschutz has been in place since 2022. This project continues to build an empowered local community for the thriving and recovered population of Sulawesi endemic wildlife in well-managed ecosystems. In 2025, We strengthened our locally led conservation for, Sulawesi flying fox (Acerodon celebensis) and Gray flying fox in Banggai, Central SulawesiForsten’s tortoise (Indotestudo forstenii), Palu to Kasimbar, Central Sulawesi and Sulawesi forest turtle (Leucocephalon yuwonoi), Palu to Kasimbar, Central SulawesiTalaud cuscus (Ailurops melanotis), Talaud island, North SulawesiRicefish and bungu endemic to Lake Poso (Adrianichthys spp. and Mugilogobius), Poso, Central SulawesiExpand our community-based conservation for, Sulawesi flying fox and Talaud CuscusBiodiversity programs, including for Sulawesi endemic fauna, such as Anoa (Bubalus spp.) and Babirusa (Babyrousa celebensis), Orchids, and Bees in Banggai and Pagimana, Central Sulawesi

Nina Elomaa
nina.elomaa@sok.fi
Foodwaste and circular economy

S Group is a customer-owned Finnish network of companies in the retail and service sectors, with approximately 2,000 outlets in Finland. S Group consists of cooperatives and SOK with its subsidiaries, which engage in the travel and hospitality business in Estonia, among other operations. S Group offers services in grocery and non-food retail, specialty retail, fuels retail and service station trade and travel industry and hospitality business. In addition, some of cooperatives belonging to S Group have car dealerships and hardware stores in their regions. S Group provides all of its co-op members with comprehensive banking services through S-Bank. S Group's aim is to halve food waste by 2030. The food waste of S Group’s grocery stores in 2024 was 1.23% (2023: 1.28). In 2024, relative food waste decreased by 4%, and the amount of waste produced was reduced by around 1,300 tonnes compared with the previous year. Compared with 2014, the amount of food waste has decreased by 31%. Red-labelled products at stores are one of the visible methods of wastage management. Products about to go out of date have been systematically discounted at S Group’s grocery stores for more than 10 years. In 2024, S Group sold a total of around 97 million (2023: 92 million) red-labelled products (-30% and -60% of the normal price). The evening discount practice has been in national use for seven years. Usable food is also donated to charity, and S Group has more than 400 food aid partners. In addition, food donations are carried out at outlets in Estonia. In addition to donations, all the 19 cooperatives use product waste boxes and bags. S Group's resource efficiency and circular economy related targets are: The goal is that the amount of waste does not increase as sales grow (decoupling = calculation method for waste amount relative to retail sales). The goal is that the ratio of waste amount to retail sales (inflation-adjusted) is 6.7 by 2030.Maintain a recycling rate of over 80% annually.The goal is to halve food waste by 2030.

The Central Union of Agricultural Producers and Forest Owners (MTK)
MTK
Anna-Rosa Asikainen
anna-rosa.asikainen@mtk.fi
MTK's and SLC's Biodiversity Road Map

MTK’s and SLC’s Biodiversity Road Map published in February 2024 is a comprehensive report on biodiversity linked to agriculture and forestry, as well as the possibilities to protect and promote it. The Road Map is based on science and research information. In addition to information describing the past and future development of biodiversity and its current state, the Road Map includes a mission, goals and measures defined by MTK and SLC. Efforts have been made to balance ecological needs on one hand and practical implementation on the other in the organisations’ statements. The mission of the Road Map is the following: “MTK and SLC together with their members work to protect and promote biodiversity in a way that halts the loss of biodiversity linked to agriculture and forestry and puts it on a path to recovery. Together with other actors, the organisations will build a stable, predictable and enabling environment, where promoting biodiversity is an inseparable part of sustainable, responsible and profitable rural livelihoods.” The goals and measures are divided into eight themes that cover organisations’ functions, the operating environment and the core aspects of agricultural and forest biodiversity. The themes are the following: 1) biodiversity in a stronger role in the activities of the organisations; 2) an enabling operating environment and cooperation as a basis; 3) safeguarding valuable habitats in commercial forests; 4) strengthening structural features valuable for biodiversity in commercial forests; 5) more diverse arable areas; 6) special attention to field margins and borders; 7) adequate and high-quality management for traditional rural biotopes and natural pastures; 8) leap forward in voluntary establishment of protected areas and restoration. The individual farmers and forest owners who form the basis of the organisations make independent decisions regarding their agriculture and forestry and thus, the goals and measures are not aimed at individual members. By implementing the Road Map, the organisations create an operating environment where individual farmers and forest owners can find suitable ways to enhance their contribution to biodiversity alongside their livelihoods. The implementation of the Road Map will proceed step by step over the coming years. Since reaching most of the goals does not depend only on the organisations themselves, many elements of the Road Map are advanced based on cooperation between MTK and SLC and different actors (e.g. authorities and administration, stakeholders).

FfB
Jasmine Khatri
jasmine.khatri@financeforbiodiversity.org; anita.de.horde@financeforbiodiversity.org; natacha.boric@financeforbiodiversity.org
Finance for Biodiversity Foundation Impact Report: From Pledge to Progress

The Finance for Biodiversity (FfB) Foundation’s first impact report marks five years since the launch of the Finance for Biodiversity (FfB) Pledge. Presenting the aggregated progress of 126 Pledge signatories against the Pledge’s five commitments of collaboration and knowledge sharing, engaging with companies, assessing impact, setting targets and reporting publicly, the report aims to support not only the FfB community but also the broader financial sector, policymakers, NGOs, data providers and civil society seeking clarity on the state of nature-related action in finance. This first Impact Report marks a new phase of transparency for the FfB community and sets the baseline for tracking progress in the years ahead. By working together, Pledge signatories and FfB members can help shift global financial flows towards a nature-positive, resilient future, particularly on Goal D and Targets 14, 15, 18 and 19.

Strengthen KMGBF implementation through actions in Socio-Ecological Production Landscapes and Seascapes "SEPLS": The Satoyama Initiative Commitment

The International Partnership for the Satoyama Initiative (IPSI) is a multistakeholder platform that promotes sustainable use of natural resources embedded in principles of equity. inclusion, and socio-ecological resilience. Launched in 2010 in response to CBD COP 10, as a joint initiative by UNU-IAS and the Ministry of Environment, Japan (MOEJ) with 51 partners to strengthen socio-ecological production landscapes and seascapes (SEPLS) the IPSI has grown to a network of more than 340 organizations including governments, NGOs, academia, financial agencies, IPLC organizations and private sector entities. The vision of the IPSI is to promote the goal of Living in Harmony with Nature. The current IPSI Strategic Plan of Action (2023-2030) was developed in line with the KMGBF and most recently an Actionable Implmentation Plan to implement the Plan of Action by the IPSI members has been adopted at the 10th IPSI Global Conference, Riobamba, Ecuador. The elements focus on strengthening implementation of area based conservation, ecosystem restoration and value addition at the level of SEPLS; and advocate through research, communication, outreach and capacity development activities the uptake of integrated approaches that allow coherent planning and decision making related to nature and people towards socio-ecological resilience. On behalf of IPSI members (IPSI Members - The International Partnership for the Satoyama Initiative (IPSI), I am happy to submit the Plan of Action (IPSI-PoA_2023-2030-1.pdf) and the Actionable Implementation Plan (a living document) as a commitment of the Partnership towards effective implementation of the KMGBF.

Veolia SA
Veolia
Tiphaine DEAS
Tiphaine.deas@veolia.com
Preserving water resources

A central pillar of Veolia's activities, the water business has guided the Group for 170 years, positioning Veolia as a leader in this sector. The Group is committed to maintaining its operational excellence while offering technological innovations to its clients, evolving from water distribution services to water science expertise. Veolia's solutions Veolia aims to become the water service provider of the future by focusing on the efficiency of its solutions, including treated wastewater reuse, water and pollutant treatment (such as PFAS and microplastics) using cutting-edge technologies, and drinking water production. Objective Preserving water resources with a target of 1.5 billion m³ of freshwater saved by 2027 through treated water reuse, desalination, and leak reduction. Developing a tool to raise consumer awareness about reducing water consumption: https://ecodeau.org/
GBF-INDICATOR-B1; GBF-INDICATOR-285; GBF-INDICATOR-200; GBF-INDICATOR-295
Elimination of phytosanitary product use on our most significant operational sites

The objective is to lead the Group to significantly reduce the use of phytosanitary products across its global scope (worlwide). 95% of sites must transition to "zero-phyto". This commitment requires a change in practices and a cultural shift both internally and externally (visitors, clients, etc.).
GBF-INDICATOR-7.2
Assessment of the environmental and biodiversity footprint of our high-stakes sites (approximately 150) to deploy biodiversity protection and restoration action plans

Veolia has been committed for many years to reducing the biodiversity impact of its sites. To achieve this, Veolia is committed to conducting biodiversity footprint assessments and deploying action plans on 100% of its sensitive sites, targeting an 85% overall action plan deployment rate by 2027. Sensitive sites, which are identified based on two major categories of criteria due to their significant biodiversity challenges. Issues related to the sensitivity and potential of the site: the type of environment in which the site is located, the presence of protected natural areas or areas of high importance for biodiversity near the site, the potential presence of threatened, endangered or protected species, land and water surface areas, and level of water stress. To this end, Veolia cross-references information extracted from the IBAT database (Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool) – developed by BirdLife International, Conservation International, the IUCN and the United Nations Environment Programme – with site geolocation data. The natural areas defined by IUCN (categories I to VI), UNESCO (natural world heritage sites), the Ramsar Convention, MAB (Man and the Biosphere Programme), the Alliance for Zero Extinction, and Important Bird and Biodiversity Areas (IBAs) are considered.Issues relating to the site's activity: air and water discharges, treatment efficiency, abstraction of resources, potential past non-compliance (discharges to the environment), and soil sealing. The assessed impacts include the consumption of natural resources during site operations (water, chemicals, biomass and energy), residual pollution from operational emissions, greenhouse gas emissions, and the effects of site land use on habitats. The involvement of an ecologist expert in local biodiversity is mandatory to support operational entities in measuring on-site footprints. The ecologist also supports the site in defining its action plan, determined according to the major issues revealed by the biodiversity footprint. This is based on a predefined list of actions aimed at reducing identified impacts and preserving or restoring nature. These actions (15 generic actions and specific actions) target the reduction of impact on habitat artificialization and fragmentation, the preservation of rare or threatened species, ecological management of green spaces, protection of water resources, control of invasive alien species, reduction of light pollution, pollution control, climate change mitigation, and stakeholder awareness. Veolia Biodiversity footprint tool approach_presentation.pdf

GBF-INDICATOR-1.1
Combat climate change

Veolia is committed to contributing to the climate challenge by acting to decarbonize our societies and help them adapt to changes already underway. The Group published an ambitious trajectory in February 2024 to achieve Net Zero by 2050, meaning zero carbon emissions from its activities and neutralization of its residual emissions. By reducing its emissions by 50% in 10 years and increasing its "avoided" emissions by 50% on scope 4: • Reduction of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions scopes 1 and 2: 2027 target -18% (vs 2021) • Avoided GHG emissions: 2027 target +30% (vs 2023) CO2 eq. Climate report Veolia
Treatment of hazardous waste

As a global leader in hazardous waste treatment with a presence on all continents, Veolia is accelerating its client activities through the deployment of unique offerings. Veolia's solutions The Group is accelerating circular economy solutions and innovation in lithium battery recycling, and is deploying large-scale plastic recycling, where Veolia is currently a leader with more than 30 plants worldwide. Objective Veolia is committed to treating 10 million tonnes of hazardous pollutant waste by 2027.
GBF-T11. Nature’s contributions to people are restored, maintained and enhanced; GBF-T16. Sustainable consumption choices are enabled, and food waste reduced by half
GBF-INDICATOR-217; GBF-INDICATOR-183
Improve water treatment efficiency

Water treatment efficiency is measured by the purification yield of wastewater treatment plants, which compares the pollutant load removed to the incoming load (in COD). For large plants (over 100,000 population equivalents), the Group aims to achieve a purification yield above 92% for COD by 2027. COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand) is a key indicator that measures the amount of oxygen required to chemically oxidize organic and inorganic matter present in water. It provides a comprehensive assessment of water pollution levels and treatment effectiveness. This indicator was chosen because it reflects the overall efficiency of wastewater treatment processes in removing pollutants, making it a reliable metric for evaluating environmental performance and compliance with regulatory standards.
GBF-T10. Areas under agriculture, aquaculture, fisheries and forestry are managed sustainably; GBF-T11. Nature’s contributions to people are restored, maintained and enhanced
GBF-INDICATOR-287
Implementation of ecological management on sites with more than 1 hectare of green spaces

Veolia is also committed to deploying ecological management on 95% of its sites with more than 1 hectare of green spaces. Sites it operates worldwide Ecological management involves the mandatory implementation of Veolia's "green spaces" charter on sites, developed in partnership with the French Committee of IUCN. Its objective is to support the transition of sites and landscape professionals towards more environmentally friendly practices (e.g., reduced mowing, planting of local species only, etc.). Zéro déforestation

Veolia commits to a "Zero deforestation" policy by maintaining 100% traceability of wood products for our energy sector and targeting 100% certified sourcing within our operational control scope of the supply chain.
The certifications obtained guarantee non-deforestation and non-conversion of wood supply sectors, as well as the exclusion of sourcing from areas of high environmental value. Certification must be compatible with the EU RED II directive (SURE, SBP, KZR INiG or PEFC).

Wetlands International
WI
Susanna Tol
susanna.tol@wetlands.org
Mangrove Breakthrough

(a) The Mangrove Breakthrough provides a framework for State and non-State Actors to work together towards global science-based goals. The Breakthrough and its partners aim to enable the mobilization of USD 4 billion by 2030, boosting actions to restore and protect 15 million hectares of mangroves — along with the communities and biodiversity that rely on them. The 2030 science-based goals of the Breakthrough are to 1) Halt Anthropogenic Mangrove Loss 2) Restore Half of Recent Losses and 3) Double the area of mangroves under formal protection. Actions to achieve this overall goal include: 1) Finance – unlocking capital for scaled impact 2) Community – progress through a global multi-stakeholder network 3) Policy – shaping global and national frameworks 4) Pipeline – scaling mangrove projects for investment and 5) Outreach – shifting the global narrative. See https://www.mangrovebreakthrough.com/actions (b) Conversion to aquaculture, oil palm plantations, and rice cultivation together explain 43% of mangrove losses between 2000 and 2020. Natural retraction, influenced by climate change, sediment shifts, and sea-level rise, also significantly impacted mangrove of areas. Regional breakdowns highlight very diverse patterns of change, with human impacts dominating change in Africa, Asia, and North and Central America. Therefore, the commitments seeks to address region, country and also site-specific drivers of mangrove loss.
Europe - Western Europe; Africa - Eastern Africa; Africa - Western Africa; Americas - Central America; Americas - Caribbean; Americas - South America; Asia - South-Eastern Asia; Asia - Southern Asia

Wetlands International, on behalf of the Freshwater Challenge Core Partners
Paula Martinelli
paula.martinelli@wetlands.org; info@freshwaterchallenge.org
The Freshwater Challenge: Restoration and Conservation of Inland Water Ecosystems

The commitment is made under the framework of the Freshwater Challenge , a country-led initiative which aims to accelerate, elevate & substantiate the restoration of 300,000 km of degraded rivers and 350 million hectares of degraded wetlands by 2030, as well as conservation of freshwater ecosystems important for biodiversity and ecosystem services. The Scope of the commitment contributes to: Restoration of degraded inland waters, more specifically rivers, streams, lakes, peatlands and other inland wetlands (measured in km and hectares); Protection and conservation of freshwater ecosystems important for biodiversity and ecosystem services, including through protected and conserved areas; Integration of freshwater ecosystem protection and restoration targets into national strategies, including NDCs, NAPs, NBSAPs, LDN targets, and wetland strategies; Alignment across Multilateral Environmental Agreements (MEAs), including CBD, UNFCCC, UNCCD and the Convention on Wetlands; Whole-of-government and whole-of-society implementation, engaging ministries, agencies, civil society, Indigenous Peoples, local communities and the private sector The commitment is embedded in a country-led process supported by FWC Core Partners - Conservation International, IUCN, Convention on Wetlands Secretariat, The Nature Conservancy, UNCCD, UNEP, WWF and Wetlands International. The key proposed actions include: Setting or strengthening quantitative targets by supporting, when requested and if resources allow it, countries Implementation of targets for freshwater ecosystem restoration and protection, also by supporting, when requested and if resources allow it, countries activities Coordination and stakeholder engagement Resource mobilisation Monitoring and learning The FWC was launched at the UN Water Conference in 2023 and it has over 50 member countries and the EU, namely: Australia, Bangladesh, Botswana, Brazil, Burkina Faso, Cambodia, Canada, Chad, Chile, Colombia, DR Congo, Dominican Republic, Ecuador, El Salvador, Fiji, France, Finland, Gabon, Gambia, Germany, Greece, Guinea, Iraq, Kazakhstan, Kenya, Liberia, Madagascar, Malawi, Mali, Mauritania, Mexico, Moldova, Mozambique, Namibia, Nepal, Netherlands, Niger, Norway, Pakistan, Panama, Peru, Republic of Congo, Senegal, Slovenia, South Africa, Spain, Tajikistan, Tanzania, UAE, Uganda, UK, Zambia and Zimbabwe. The timeline of the FWC commitment is from 2023-2030, with continued implementation envisaged beyond 2030.
Wetlands International, on behalf of the Peatland Breakthrough
Laura MacKenzie
laura.mackenzie@wetlands.org; eva.hernandez@wetlands.org
Peatland Breakthrough

The Peatland Breakthrough is a collaborative initiative to mobilize and accelerate action on peatlands. It provides a framework for State and non-State Actors to work together towards global science-based goals. The Breakthrough and its partners aim to enable the mobilization of USD 100 billion by 2030, boosting actions to halt peatland destruction, restore and rewet 30 million hectares of peatlands and enable sustainable and wise use in all peatlands by 2050. The Peatland Breakthrough has defined a Global science-based target framework for peatlands, including 3 core targets: By 2030, halt the anthropogenic loss of undrained peatland By 2030, at least 30 million hectares of degraded peatlands are being rewetted and restored By 2030, enabling conditions for sustainable, wise use are developed, and by 2050, it is implemented on all peatland.These core targets shall allow the delivery of target 4: By 2050, the global peatland area has reached net zero emissions and preferably is a net GHG sink, supporting climate-resilient pathways. By 2030, be clearly on track through core Targets 1, 2 and 3. And the previous targets will be enabled by targets 5 and 6: 5) Mobilize at least USD 100 billion by 2030 to operationalize peatland conservation, rewetting and restoration, and sustainable, wise use; 6) By 2030, all countries with significant peatland-related emissions have established or maintained national peatland inventories and monitoring systems aligned with international standards. By 2050, a mature, global peatland monitoring architecture is in place. Actions to achieve these targets include: Building a strong and diverse community of entities and communities supporting peatland action;Creating enabling conditions for action at scale e.g. in terms of public and private policies, actionable knowledge, and awareness raising; Mobilising finance and Delivering action on the ground at different scales, while monitoring progress. Which direct and indirect drivers or enabling conditions of biodiversity loss the commitment will seek to address At least 12% of peatlands globally have been degraded, mainly due to drainage for agriculture and forestry, including oil palm and acacia plantations. Other causes of degradation are overgrazing of livestock, mining for fuel and horticulture, pollution by human activity and infrastructure development; many are deliberately burned. The actions stemming from the Peatland Breakthrough, being a global call to action, shall help tackle different causes of destruction depending on the specific context.
