Wetlands International, on behalf of the Rights of Wetlands partnership
laura.mackenzie@wetlands.org
Advancing Rights of Wetlands

Overview of the Rights of Wetlands Wetlands are a key ecosystem for our survival, of unparalleled importance for biodiversity, climate, and ecosystem services - particularly water quantity and quality. Yet wetlands and their biodiversity continue to be lost through infilling and drainage for agriculture and urban settlements, and degraded by pollution, alterations to water regimes, fragmentation, climate change, and over-extraction of natural resources. In 2021, a group including wetland and climate scientists, attorneys, and others developed the Universal Declaration of the Rights of Wetlands and formed the Rights of Wetlands initiative. Rights of Wetlands recognises that new approaches to how we view and interact with nature are required to address biodiversity loss and climate destabilisation, which pose an existential threat to wildlife, people and planet. Efforts to protect nature through conservation actions and environmental protection are proving insufficient. Rights of Wetlands is part of a global Rights of Nature movement, which is transformative and shifts the globally-predominant paradigm of exploitation, extraction, and depletion that is responsible for endangering the wellbeing and existence of Nature. Rights of Nature reconfigures the human-wetlands relationship from one of exploitation, extraction, and depletion to one that recognizes the Rights and living beingness of wetlands, and is based on reciprocity, kinship, and gratitude. Rights of Wetlands is about behavioural and systems change through legal frameworks and through how communities and individuals interact with the wetlands they live next to or within. Rights of Wetlands addresses what IPBES identifies as indirect drivers of ecosystem loss and degradation, and the social values and behaviours which underpin them, action on which is essential to achieve KMGBF goals and targets (including those specifically referencing inland waters). Rights of Wetlands also aligns with the recognition in the KMGBF of diverse value systems and concepts, including, for those countries that recognise them, rights of nature and rights of Mother Earth, as being an integral part of its successful implementation, and including the mention in Target 19(f) of ‘Mother Earth centric actions’. The Universal Declaration of the Rights of Wetlands declares that all wetlands are entities entitled to inherent and enduring rights, which derive from their existence as members of the Earth community and should possess legal standing in courts of law. These rights include: The right to existThe right to their ecologically determined location in the landscapeThe right to natural, connected and sustainable hydrological regimesThe right to ecologically sustainable climatic conditionsThe right to have naturally occurring biodiversity, free of introduced or invasive species that disrupt their ecological integrityThe right to integrity of structure, function, evolutionary processes and the ability to fulfil natural ecological roles in the Earth's processesThe right to be free from pollution and degradationThe right to regeneration and restorationActions of the Rights of Wetlands partnership commitment Building on the three-year project ‘Rights of wetlands operationalisation for biodiversity and community resilience’ (2023-2026) that was generously funded by the UK Government's Darwin Initiative, this commitment comprises of the following actions: a) Support local communities and individuals who want to implement Rights of Wetlands to take actions to: Recognise different human-nature worldviewsDevelop or influence local planning to secure recognition of Rights of WetlandsDevelop a truth reckoning and right-relationship processEmpower Rights of Wetlands community actionProvide Rights of Wetlands educationNetwork with others in Rights of Wetlands communityb) Support local communities and individuals who are already implementing Rights of Wetlands by: Encouraging others to support their approachesAdvocating for land and resource rights for their territoryCombining traditional and science knowledge to support legal defenceFacilitating networking with others in Rights of Wetlands community Community support will be delivered through capacity building, toolkits, trainings, and sharing of community experience through participatory video. c) Support governments to: Include Rights of Wetlands in constitution, national law or policyInclude Rights of Wetlands in local laws, policies and plansAdvocate for Rights of Wetlands in intergovernmental forums and support the adoption of a Rights of Wetlands resolution at the Convention on Wetlands COP16 in Panama 2028.Support to governments will be delivered through capacity building, toolkits and online and in-person workshops.

Wildlife Conservation Society
WCS
Alfred DeGemmis
adegemmis@wcs.org; slieberman@wcs.org
Protecting Climate-Resilient Coral Reefs: A High-Level Global Commitment

The Climate-Resilient Coral Reefs Commitment is a high-level global pledge, first launched at the Third United Nations Ocean Conference (Nice, June 2025), to protect the world's most climate-resilient coral reefs — those with the greatest potential to survive and support reef ecosystem recovery in a warming world. As of December 2025, 15 governments have signed the Commitment, alongside more than 20 global partner organizations.
Scope and actions proposed: Signatory governments and supporting organizations commit to the following actions: ● Integrate coral reef conservation into national biodiversity and climate strategies, including NBSAPs, NDCs, and relevant development plans, ensuring alignment with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework and the Paris Agreement. ● Prioritize climate-resilient reefs within 30x30 commitments by identifying and protecting reefs most likely to persist and serve as recovery sources for the future. ● Strengthen national coral reef action plans and monitoring systems, building on existing mechanisms to reduce local threats, measure progress, and guide adaptive management. ● Enact and enforce policies to reduce land-based pollution, eliminate destructive and illegal fishing, and manage coastal development to minimize cumulative stress on reef ecosystems. ● Mobilize financing and build capacity, with emphasis on community leadership, Indigenous and traditional knowledge, and technology transfer. ● Partner with Indigenous people and local communities, as well as other stakeholders, to ensure that coral reef solutions are equitable, inclusive, and grounded in place-based stewardship.
Drivers of biodiversity loss addressed: The Commitment directly addresses climate change as the primary driver of coral reef decline, as well as the indirect and direct drivers of overfishing and destructive fishing practices, land-based pollution (sedimentation, nutrient runoff), and unsustainable coastal development. It also promotes the enabling conditions of equitable governance, Indigenous and local community stewardship, science-based management, and mobilization of biodiversity finance.
Australia; Bahamas (The); Belize; Indonesia; Madagascar; Maldives; Panama; Papua New Guinea; United Republic of Tanzania; France; Vanuatu; Brazil; Solomon Islands; Palau; Fiji

Working Group ICCAs Indonesia (Gerakan Konservasi Rakyat Nusantara)
WGII
Mega Ayu Lestari
sekretariat.wgii@gmail.com; cindy.wgii@gmail.com; utterly.mega@gmail.com
Strengthening Tenure Rights and Defending Indigenous Peoples and Local Community-led Conservation through the Lens of ICCA

There is a critical point where weaving together insights from across diverse knowledge systems and approaches, including Indigenous and local knowledge, contributes to enhancing strategies and actions for transformative change (IPBES Thematic Assessment Report, 2025). At Working Group ICCAs Indonesia (WGII), we foster collective and collaborative endeavors through three primary domains of work. First, is the Documenting of ICCAs, which focuses on recording and fortifying the traditional ecological management and biodiversity protection systems practiced by Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities (IP&LCs). Second is the Defending of ICCAs, encompassing advocacy for the recognition of IP&LCs rights, alongside efforts to influence conservation policies to ensure they are anchored in human rights principles and social justice at the local, national, and international levels. Within this context, the ICCA framework positions Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities (IP&LCs) not as passive subjects, but as the principal agents of their conservation efforts as a social movement. Third is the Sustaining of ICCAs, aimed at empowering these communities as legitimate rights-holders and knowledge custodians of their lands and natural resources, while concurrently enhancing their capacity to access and derive sustainable benefits from resource governance. This empirically substantiates that community-led approaches constitute the most effective and equitable mechanisms for safeguarding ecosystem integrity. The richness of Indonesia's biodiversity is inextricably linked to its socio-cultural diversity, wherein IP&LCs hold a central role as territorial custodians and bearers of traditional knowledge systems. For over eleven years, WGII has documented the conservation practices undertaken by IP&LCs grounded in their customary laws (adat), culture, value and belief systems, and traditional ecological knowledge, which we refer to as ICCAs; Territories of Life (Territories and Areas Conserved by Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities). The documentation of ICCAs in Indonesia constitutes a movement initiated by WGII in collaboration with grassroots communities to render visible a diverse and rich conservation practices that have long existed and been operationalized by these communities. Simultaneously, this initiative catalyzes a paradigm shift, asserting that biodiversity protection and the utilization and governance of natural resources cannot be divorced from human rights and the rights of the communities who have inhabited, managed, and stewarded their territories intergenerationally. The vision of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KM-GBF) reinforces the impetus for a global conservation paradigm shift. Colonial conservation models, which emphasize the sterilization of landscapes and the artificial separation of humans from nature, are increasingly viewed as irrelevant, often exacerbating socio-ecological crises. It is at this juncture that a rights-based approach is increasingly affirmed as the foundational pillar of biodiversity conservation. Biodiversity protection cannot solely rely on spatial metrics or numerical targets; it must proceed in accordance with the recognition, protection, respect, and fulfillment of IP&LC rights. Consequently, WGII’s commitment focuses on strengthening the ICCA data system through a National Registry system at the iccas.or.id portal as an instrument to substantiate the recognition of rights over customary territories and community-managed areas, thereby enhancing tenure security. The development of the ICCA National Registry serves as a strategic intervention and contribution to increase visibility and enable recognition of IP&LC contributions to biodiversity conservation. Furthermore, this commitment encompasses capacity building within the ICCA Rights-Holders Network (Jaringan Pemangku Hak (JPH) AKKM), to ensure that territorial rights-holders possess the technical, institutional, and advocacy capabilities required to manage data, campaign for the recognition of tenurial rights, and participate meaningfully in biodiversity policy processes at both national and global scales. In 2023, WGII initiated the ICCA Rights-Holders Network, a platform that convenes Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities who actively govern and safeguard their customary territories.
Drivers or enabling conditions of biodiversity loss (optional) The potential disenfranchisement and loss of tenurial rights in terrestrial and waters areas. coupled with the threat of land grabbing, land conversion, tenure conflict, including human rights violation;Expansion of land based investment in Indonesia within Indigenous and traditional territories including within their ICCAs through a variety type investment including infrastructure, foreign investments, plantation, mining and energy transition agenda;The severe risks and vulnerabilities confronting Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities (IP&LCs) are compounded by the ineffective enactment of Free, Prior, and Informed Consent (FPIC) Principal in the implementation of as such land-based investment;Biocultural violence has emerged as a potent threat endangering IP&LCs. A salient manifestation of this is human-wildlife conflict (such as in the Kampar River region), which is precipitated by the fragmentation and constriction of tiger corridors;Lack of tenure rights recognition for Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities territories due to a fragmented, multi-layered bureaucratic apparatus divided along rigid sectoral lines has systematically eroded the community's legal standing in the face of aggressive development agendas.

Living with Big Cats Initiative

An overview of scope, and the actions proposed;The Living with Big Cats (LBC) Initiative supports governments, WWF country offices and partners to ensure populations of big cats in priority landscapes are stable or increasing while communities experience improved well-being through coexistence. The initiative strengthens delivery systems and strengthens project implementation by: Designing and scaling tested human–wildlife coexistence approaches (e.g. C2C: Conflict to Coexistence Approach);Supporting community stewardship and benefit mechanisms (e.g.. Wildlife Credits);Improving habitat connectivity and land-use planning (e.g. big cat-specific linear infrastructure mitigation);Strengthening partnerships and landscape coalitions (e.g. coordination of the Kenya-Tanzania Borderlands Carnivore Conservation Coalition, Panthera partnership, etc.);Enhancing knowledge sharing and technical capacity (e.g. C2C Training of trainers, webinars, landscape exchange meetings, etc.);Supporting funding mobilization and policy engagement (e.g. proposal support and leadership of the big cat policy working group) The initiative works across multiple landscapes globally to ensure investments and lessons in one landscape benefit others, reducing duplication and accelerating impact. The initiative addresses key drivers including: Human–wildlife conflict and retaliatory killingHabitat loss and fragmentationUnsustainable land use and infrastructure expansionLimited local benefits from wildlife conservationWeak coordination and capacity for coexistence managementBy strengthening coexistence and stewardship systems, the initiative reduces pressure on species while improving community resilience.

Freshwater Challenge; Restoration and Conservation of Inland Waters

The commitment is made under the framework of the Freshwater Challenge initiative, which aims to accelerate, elevate & substantiate the restoration of 300,000 km of degraded rivers and 350 million hectares of degraded wetlands by 2030, as well as conserve intact freshwater ecosystems The Scope of the commitment contributes to: Restoration of degraded inland waters, more specifically rivers and wetlands (measured in km and hectares);Protection and conservation of intact freshwater ecosystems;Integration of freshwater targets into national strategies, including NDCs, NAPs, NBSAPs, LDN targets, and wetland strategies;Alignment across Multilateral Environmental Agreements (MEAs), including CBD, UNFCCC, UNCCD and the Convention on Wetlands;Whole-of-government and whole-of-society implementation, engaging ministries, agencies, civil society, Indigenous Peoples, local communities and the private sectorThe commitment is embedded in a country-led process supported by FWC Core Partners - Conservation International, IUCN, Convention on Wetlands Secretariat, TNC, UNCCD, UNEP, WWF and Wetlands International. The key proposed actions include: Setting or strengthening quantitative targets by supporting, when requested and if resources allow it, countriesImplementation of targets for restoration and protection, also by supporting, when requested and if resources allow it, countries activitiesCoordination and stakeholder engagementResource mobilisationMonitoring and learning

Zoologischer Garten Berlin AG
Katharina Herrmann
k.hermann@zoo-berlin.de
Ex-situ management of captive populations and reintroduction

Zoo and Tierpark Berlin implement integrated ex situ and in situ conservation measures aimed at supporting the long-term recovery of threatened species within their natural ranges. As members of the World Association of Zoos and Aquariums, the European Association of Zoos and Aquaria and the German Zoo Association, both institutions participate in internationally coordinated conservation breeding and population management programmes, including EAZA Ex Situ Programmes (EEP), International Studbooks (ISB) and Global Species Management Plans (GSMP). These programmes are scientifically managed to: maintain genetically viable and demographically stable conservation populationssafeguard genetic diversity as a resource for species recoverysupport conservation translocations, including reintroduction and population reinforcementcontribute to long-term species survival strategiesZoo and Tierpark Berlin currently participate in 240 EEPs and coordinate 10 of them, providing expertise in population planning, husbandry, veterinary care and conservation biology. Ex situ management is closely linked to in situ conservation efforts. Over the past ten years, more than 340 captive-bred individuals have been released into suitable natural habitats in cooperation with governmental authorities and conservation partners, with the objective of establishing or strengthening self-sustaining wild populations. Examples include: Translocation of European bison to Azerbaijan for release into Shahdag National Park in partnership with WWF Germany, IDEA and the Ministry of Ecology and Natural Resources of Azerbaijan.Translocation of Przewalski’s horses to Kazakhstan in cooperation with Prague Zoo, Zoological Society Frankfurt and national partners.Contribution of bearded vultures to the European reintroduction programme coordinated by the Vulture Conservation Foundation.Reintroduction of European hamster populations in Saxony, Germany, under the leadership of Zoo Leipzig.All conservation translocations are conducted according to internationally recognised IUCN guidelines and in alignment with national species conservation strategies. These interventions are species-focused, implemented within existing ecological frameworks, and designed to stabilise and strengthen wild populations. While targeting species recovery, they may also support ecological integrity and generate co-benefits for other species sharing the same ecosystems.

Public Environmental Education – Knowledge, Responsibility, and Action?

About 5.5 million visitors from around the world come to Zoo and Tierpark Berlin each year. The institutions aim to excite people about biodiversity and its protection by creating positive experiences in a natural setting, attractive to all age groups. The environmental education programme includes: Zoo- and Tierpark Education Centres, which have been recognised by the United Nations Decade on Biodiversity (2011–2020). Education measures include signage throughout the parks, keeper talks, and the „Zoo- and Tierpark School”, an inhouse educational institution offering guided tours and interactive animal-based experiences.Around 5,000 guided tours are conducted annually, reaching approximately 48,000 participants. The programme also offers interactive sessions aligned with the Berlin school curriculum. Outdoor lessons on the Nature Trail allow students to learn hands-on about urban nature, the roles of insects, amphibians, deadwood, and the connections between food, farming, and biodiversity.Since 2021, under the patronage of the Federal Ministry for the Environment and as part of the UN Decade on Ecosystem Restoration (2021–2030), Zoo and Tierpark Berlin invite Berlin schoolchildren and youth to submit project ideas for a greener city. The prize, worth €20,000, supports independent implementation and encourages young people to actively shape a sustainable and nature-friendly urban environment.
International Project Support

Zoo and Tierpark Berlin are part of a global network of like-minded institutions and are involved in meaningful nature conservation partnerships. Around 30 nature conservation projects around the globe are supported by Zoo and Tierpark Berlin's “Berlin World Wild” nature conservation program. The focus is on strengthening the local capacities of partners on the ground to halt and reverse biodiversity loss. This includes the targeted promotion of activities focusing on environmental education, research, and species and habitat protection. The financial and in-kind support is provided based on the individual projects’ needs. In-kind support includes for example, the exchange of expertise on veterinary care and the management of captive-bred animals for reintroduction. An overview of current conservation projects can be found on: https://www.zoo-berlin.de/en/species-conservation/worldwide and https://www.tierpark-berlin.de/en/species-conservation/worldwide.
